title.zh: 卷四十五 袁張韓周列傳
title.en: Volume 45: Biographies of Yuan, Zhang, Han, Zhou
title.raw: 卷四十五 袁張韓周列傳 Volume 45: Biographies of Yuan, Zhang, Han, Zhou

sentences.zh: 袁安字邵公，汝南汝陽人也。

translations.literal: Yuan An, courtesy name Shaogong, was a man of Runan commandery, Ruyang.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An, whose courtesy name was Shaogong, came from Ruyang in Runan.

sentences.zh: 祖父良，習孟氏易，[一]平帝時舉明經，為太子舍人；

translations.literal: His grandfather Liang studied the Meng school of the Changes; in the time of Emperor Ping he was recommended on the classics and became a gentleman attendant at the heir apparent's residence;
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Yuan Liang had mastered the Meng tradition of the Book of Changes; under Emperor Ping he was nominated for his classical learning and appointed gentleman attendant at the crown prince's residence;

sentences.zh: [二]建武初，至成武令。

translations.literal: [2] In the early Jianwu period he reached the office of magistrate of Chengwu.
translations.idiomatic: Early in the Jianwu era he was promoted to magistrate of Chengwu.
Editorial note [3].

sentences.zh: 注[一]孟喜字長卿，東海人。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Meng Xi, courtesy name Changqing, was a man of Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Meng Xi, style Changqing, was from Donghai.

sentences.zh: 明易，為丞相掾。

translations.literal: He understood the Changes and became a clerk in the chancellor's office.
translations.idiomatic: He was versed in the Changes and served as a clerk under the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 見前書。

translations.literal: See the earlier history.
translations.idiomatic: See the account in the Former Han history.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢志曰：「太子舍人，秩二百石，無員。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: The Continued Han Monograph says: "Gentlemen attendants at the heir apparent's residence, rank two hundred piculs, no fixed quota."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: According to the Continued Han Monograph, gentlemen attendants at the crown prince's residence held rank at two hundred piculs and had no fixed quota.

sentences.zh: 注[三]成武，今曹州縣。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Chengwu is today a county in Cao prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Chengwu is the present-day county under Cao prefecture.

sentences.zh: 安少傳良學。

translations.literal: In his youth An transmitted Liang's learning.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man Yuan An carried on his grandfather's scholarship.

sentences.zh: 為人嚴重有威，見敬於州裡。

translations.literal: In his person he was stern and weighty and had authority; he was respected in his village.
translations.idiomatic: He was grave and dignified, carried natural authority, and earned the respect of his neighbors.

sentences.zh: 初為縣功曹，[一]奉檄詣從事，從事因安致書於令。

translations.literal: At first he was merit assessor of the county; [1] carrying a dispatch he went to the clerk of the headquarters, and the clerk had An deliver a private letter to the magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: He began as county merit assessor; [1] when he carried an official dispatch to the provincial clerk, that clerk tried to have him pass a private letter to the magistrate.

sentences.zh: [二]安曰：「公事自有郵驛，私請則非功曹所持。

translations.literal: [2] An said: "Public business has its postal relays; private requests are not what the merit assessor may carry.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Yuan An replied, "Official business travels by the post; a personal favor is not something a merit assessor should handle.

sentences.zh: 」辭不肯受，從事懼然而止。

translations.literal: " He declined and would not accept; the clerk was afraid and desisted.
translations.idiomatic: " He refused to take the letter, and the clerk, alarmed, dropped the matter.

sentences.zh: [三]後舉孝廉，[四]除陰平長﹑任城令，[五]所在吏人畏而愛之。

translations.literal: [3] Later he was recommended as filial and incorrupt; [4] he was appointed magistrate of Yinping and of Rencheng; [5] wherever he went clerks and common people feared yet loved him.
translations.idiomatic: [3] He was later nominated as filial and incorrupt; [4] he was appointed magistrate of Yinping and then of Rencheng; [5] in both places officials and commoners stood in awe of him and loved him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢志曰：「縣功曹史，主選署功勞。」

translations.literal: Comment [1]: The Continued Han Monograph says: "The county merit assessor oversaw selection and recording of merit."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Continued Han Monograph states that the county merit assessor was responsible for evaluating and registering merit.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢志曰：「每州刺史皆有從事史。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: The Continued Han Monograph says: "Each provincial governor had clerks of the headquarters."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Continued Han Monograph records that every provincial governor had headquarters clerks.

sentences.zh: 注[三]懼音九具反。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: The gloss gives the fanqie spelling for the character in the lemma, initial from the syllable spelled jiu, final from ju.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The commentary supplies the standard fanqie reading for the character glossed in the lemma.

sentences.zh: 注[四]汝南先賢傳曰「時大雪積地丈餘，洛陽令身出案行，見人家皆除雪出，有乞食者。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: The Worthies of Runan says: "At that time snow had piled more than a zhang deep; the magistrate of Luoyang went out in person to inspect and saw every household clearing snow and coming out, and there were beggars.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Worthies of Runan relates that snow lay more than ten feet deep; the magistrate of Luoyang went out on patrol and saw families shoveling paths into the street, among them people begging for food.

sentences.zh: 至袁安門，無有行路。

translations.literal: When he came to Yuan An's gate there was no cleared path.
translations.idiomatic: At Yuan An's door no path had been cleared through the snow.

sentences.zh: 謂安己死，令人除雪入戶，見安僵臥。

translations.literal: He thought An was already dead and ordered men to clear the snow and enter the house, and saw An lying stiff.
translations.idiomatic: Assuming Yuan An had frozen to death, he had the snow dug away and entered, only to find Yuan An lying rigid inside.

sentences.zh: 問何以不出。

translations.literal: He asked why he did not go out.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate asked why he had not come outdoors.

sentences.zh: 安曰：『大雪人皆餓，不宜干人。

translations.literal: An said: "In great snow everyone is hungry; it is not fitting to trouble others.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An said, "In this snow everyone is starving; I should not impose on others.

sentences.zh: 』令以為賢，舉為孝廉」也。

translations.literal: " The magistrate thought him worthy and recommended him as filial and incorrupt."
translations.idiomatic: " The magistrate deemed him worthy and nominated him as filial and incorrupt."

sentences.zh: 注[五]陰平，縣，故城在今沂州承縣西南。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: Yinping was a county; its old walled town is southwest of Cheng county in Yi prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Yinping was a county whose old seat lay southwest of present Cheng county in Yi prefecture.

sentences.zh: 任城，今兗州縣也。

translations.literal: Rencheng is today a county in Yan prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Rencheng is now a county under Yan prefecture.

sentences.zh: 永平十三年，楚王英謀為逆，事下郡覆考。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year of Yongping, the king of Chu, Ying, plotted rebellion; the matter was sent down to the commandery for repeated examination.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 13, King Ying of Chu plotted treason; the case was referred to the commandery for reinvestigation.

sentences.zh: 明年，三府舉安能理劇，拜楚郡太守。

translations.literal: The next year the Three Offices recommended An as able to manage difficult cases, and he was appointed grand administrator of Chu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the Three Offices nominated Yuan An for his skill with complex litigation, and he was named grand administrator of Chu.

sentences.zh: 是時英辭所連及系者數千人，顯宗怒甚，吏案之急，迫痛自誣，死者甚觿。

translations.literal: At that time those whom Ying's confession implicated and who were bound numbered several thousand; Emperor Xianzong was extremely angry; clerks pressed the case harshly; under torture they falsely confessed; the dead were exceedingly many.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands had been tied to the case by King Ying's confession; Emperor Ming was furious, and his clerks drove the interrogations so hard that people tortured themselves into false confessions, and deaths piled up in great number.

sentences.zh: 安到郡，不入府，先往案獄，理其無明驗者，條上出之。

translations.literal: When An reached the commandery he did not enter the headquarters but first went to review the prisons and sorted out those without clear evidence, listing them and memorializing to release them.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An went straight to the jails instead of the yamen, reviewed the prisoners, separated out those without solid evidence, and memorialized in detail to have them freed.

sentences.zh: 府丞掾史皆叩頭爭，以為阿附反虜，法與同罪，不可。

translations.literal: The assistant administrator and the clerks all knocked their heads in protest, saying that to curry favor with rebels was the same crime in law and could not be done.
translations.idiomatic: The assistant and the staff kowtowed in alarm, insisting that pandering to traitors made them liable under the same statute and must not be allowed.

sentences.zh: 安曰：「如有不合，太守自當坐之，不以相及也。

translations.literal: An said: "If there is anything improper, the grand administrator himself will bear it and will not drag you in.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An said, "If anything is wrong, I as grand administrator will answer for it myself; I will not shift the blame onto you.

sentences.zh: 」遂分別具奏。

translations.literal: " Then he separately prepared a full memorial.
translations.idiomatic: " He then drew up separate memorials for each category.

sentences.zh: 帝感悟，即報許，得出者四百餘家。

translations.literal: The emperor was moved and immediately replied granting permission; more than four hundred households were able to leave.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was persuaded, approved at once, and over four hundred families were released.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，征為河南尹。

translations.literal: After more than a year he was summoned to be governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later he was recalled to serve as governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 政號嚴明，然未曾以臧罪鞠人。

translations.literal: His government was called strict and clear, yet he never on a corruption charge interrogated a man.
translations.idiomatic: His administration was known for severity and clarity, yet he never hauled anyone in on a graft charge.

sentences.zh: 常稱曰：「凡學仕者，高則望宰相，下則希牧守。

translations.literal: He often said: "Those who study to become officials, high ones hope to be chancellor, low ones hope to be a shepherd or grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: He was fond of saying, "Men who enter office through study aim high at the chancellorship or, more modestly, at a governorship or grand administrator post.

sentences.zh: 錮人於聖世，尹所不忍為也。

translations.literal: To imprison people in a sage age is what the governor cannot bear to do.
translations.idiomatic: To lock people away in an enlightened reign is something I as governor cannot bring myself to do.

sentences.zh: 」聞之者皆感激自勵。

translations.literal: " Those who heard it were all moved and urged themselves on.
translations.idiomatic: " All who heard him were stirred and strove to live up to his example.

sentences.zh: 在職十年，京師肅然，名重朝廷。

translations.literal: In office ten years, the capital was orderly; his name weighed heavily at court.
translations.idiomatic: During ten years in the post the capital stayed orderly, and his reputation at court was immense.

sentences.zh: 建初八年，遷太僕。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of Jianchu he was transferred to grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianchu 8 he was promoted to grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 元和二年，武威太守孟雲上書：「北虜既已和親，而南部復往抄掠，北單于謂漢欺之，謀欲犯邊。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanhe, grand administrator Meng Yun of Wuwei memorialized: "The northern barbarians have already made peace and alliance, yet the southern division again goes raiding; the northern shanyu says Han deceives him and plans to violate the border.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanhe 2, Meng Yun, grand administrator of Wuwei, submitted a memorial: "The northern tribes are at peace with us, yet the southern court keeps raiding them; the northern shanyu believes we are betraying him and intends to strike the frontier.

sentences.zh: 宜還其生口，以安慰之。

translations.literal: It is fitting to return their captives alive in order to console them.
translations.idiomatic: We ought to return the border captives we hold so as to reassure them.

sentences.zh: 」詔百官議朝堂。

translations.literal: " An edict ordered the hundred officials to deliberate in the court hall.
translations.idiomatic: " The court ordered the high officials to debate the matter in the audience hall.

sentences.zh: 公卿皆言夷狄譎詐，求欲無猒，[一]既得生口，當復妄自誇大，不可開許。

translations.literal: The dukes and ministers all said the Yi and Di are deceitful and their desires know no satiety; [1] once they have the captives alive they will again vainly boast and enlarge themselves; it cannot be opened and granted.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers argued that the barbarians were treacherous and insatiable; [1] if the captives were sent back they would only swagger and make fresh demands, so the door must not be opened.

sentences.zh: 安獨曰：「北虜遣使奉獻和親，有得邊生口者，輒以歸漢，此明其畏威，而非先違約也。

translations.literal: An alone said: "The northern barbarians sent envoys with tribute and sought peace and alliance; whenever they obtained captives from the border they at once returned them to Han; this shows clearly that they fear our might and did not first break the treaty.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An alone said, "The northerners have sent tribute and sought peace; whenever they took Han subjects on the frontier they handed them back at once. That shows they respect our power and did not break the pact first.

sentences.zh: 雲以大臣典邊，不宜負信於戎狄，還之足示中國優貸，而使邊人得安，誠便。

translations.literal: Yun as a great minister in charge of the border ought not to break faith with the Rong and Di; returning them suffices to show the Middle Kingdom's generous indulgence and lets border people be secure; it is truly expedient.
translations.idiomatic: A frontier minister like Meng Yun should not break faith with the tribes; returning the captives would display the empire's largesse, calm the border, and plainly serve our interest.

sentences.zh: 」司徒桓虞改議從安。

translations.literal: " Minister of education Huan Yu changed the discussion and followed An.
translations.idiomatic: " Minister of education Huan Yu switched sides and backed Yuan An.

sentences.zh: 太尉鄭弘﹑司空第五倫皆恨之。

translations.literal: Grand commandant Zheng Hong and minister of works the Fifth Lun both resented it.
translations.idiomatic: Grand commandant Zheng Hong and minister of works Fifth Lun both resented him for it.

sentences.zh: 弘因大言激勵虞曰：「諸言當還生口者，皆為不忠。

translations.literal: Hong therefore spoke loudly to incite Yu, saying: "All who say the captives should be returned are disloyal.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Hong then shouted to provoke Huan Yu: "Anyone who says we should return the captives is disloyal.

sentences.zh: 」虞廷叱之，倫及大鴻臚韋彪各作色變容，司隸校尉舉奏，安等皆上印綬謝。

translations.literal: " Yu rebuked him in the courtyard; Lun and the grand herald Wei Biao each changed color; the metropolitan commandant memorialized; An and the others all offered their seals and ribbons to apologize.
translations.idiomatic: " Huan Yu dressed him down in court; Fifth Lun and grand herald Wei Biao flushed with anger; the metropolitan commandant filed charges, and Yuan An and his allies all tendered their seals in apology.

sentences.zh: 肅宗詔報曰：「久議沉滯，各有所志。

translations.literal: Emperor Suzong's edict replied: "The long debate has sunk in stagnation; each has his own intent.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang's rescript read, "This dispute has dragged on because every man clings to his own view.

sentences.zh: 蓋事以議從，策由觿定，誾誾衎衎，得禮之容，[二]寢嘿抑心，更非朝廷之福。

translations.literal: Generally matters succeed when they follow counsel; policy is fixed when the assembled view is settled; earnest and harmonious debate is the countenance of ritual; [2] to lie silent and suppress the heart is again not the fortune of the court.
translations.idiomatic: Affairs advance when counsel is heeded and policy settles once minds meet in debate; frank, respectful argument is what ritual commends; [2] forced silence and bottled resentment bode ill for the court.

sentences.zh: 君何尤而深謝？

translations.literal: Why do you blame yourselves and apologize so deeply?
translations.idiomatic: Why should you fault yourselves and apologize so abjectly?

sentences.zh: 其各冠履。

translations.literal: Let each put on cap and shoes.
translations.idiomatic: Put your caps and shoes back on and resume your posts.

sentences.zh: 」帝竟從安議。

translations.literal: " The emperor in the end followed An's proposal.
translations.idiomatic: " In the end the emperor adopted Yuan An's recommendation.

sentences.zh: 明年，代第五倫為司空。

translations.literal: The next year he replaced the Fifth Lun as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he succeeded Fifth Lun as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 章和元年，代桓虞為司徒。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhanghe he replaced Huan Yu as minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhanghe 1 he replaced Huan Yu as minister of education.

sentences.zh: 注[一]譎亦詐也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: The gloss says the word here means deceitful, the same sense as the usual word for fraud.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The commentary explains the word as deceitful, synonymous with the common term for fraud.

sentences.zh: 注[二]誾誾，忠正貌。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: The reduplication describes the bearing of loyal, forthright argument.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The repeated syllables describe earnest, loyal forthrightness in debate.

sentences.zh: 衎衎，和樂貌。

translations.literal: The reduplication describes a relaxed, harmonious manner.
translations.idiomatic: The paired syllables describe a genial, harmonious demeanor.

sentences.zh: 唯安獨與任隗守正不移，至免冠朝堂固爭者十上。

translations.literal: Only An together with Ren Kui held to rectitude and did not shift; they went so far as to remove their caps in the court hall and firmly disputed, memorializing more than ten times.
translations.idiomatic: Only Yuan An and Ren Kui held their ground; they even removed their caps in the hall of state and argued tenaciously, memorializing again and again.

sentences.zh: 太后不聽，觿皆為之危懼，安正色自若。

translations.literal: The empress dowager would not listen; all were fearful for them; An with correct color was self-possessed.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager ignored them; everyone feared for their lives, yet Yuan An kept his composure and an even countenance.

sentences.zh: 竇憲既出，而弟衛尉篤﹑執金吾景各專威權，公於京師使客遮道奪人財物。

translations.literal: After Dou Xian had gone out, his younger brother the commandant of the guards Du and the bearer of the golden mace Jing each monopolized awesome power, and in the capital they openly sent retainers to block the road and seize people's goods.
translations.idiomatic: Once Dou Xian had left the capital, his brother Dou Du as commandant of the guards and Dou Jing as bearer of the golden mace each wielded unchecked power; their agents openly waylaid people in Luoyang and seized their property.

sentences.zh: 景又□使乘驛施檄緣邊諸郡，發突騎及善騎射有才力者，漁陽﹑鴈門﹑上谷三郡各遣吏將送詣景第。

translations.literal: Jing also □ used the post relays to circulate orders along the border commanderies, calling out picked cavalry and men skilled in riding and shooting who had strength and talent; Yuyang, Yanmen, and Shanggu each sent clerks leading them to deliver them to Jing's residence.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Jing also abused the courier network to circulate orders along the frontier, drafting crack cavalry and expert bowmen; the three commanderies of Yuyang, Yanmen, and Shanggu each dispatched officers to march these men to Dou Jing's mansion.

sentences.zh: 有司畏憚，莫敢言者。

translations.literal: The responsible officials feared them and there was none who dared speak.
translations.idiomatic: The bureaus were too intimidated to report any of it.

sentences.zh: 安乃劾景□發邊兵，驚惑吏人，二千石不待符信而輒承景檄，當伏顯誅。

translations.literal: An then impeached Jing □ for mobilizing border troops, alarming clerks and common people, and for two-thousand-picul officials who without tallies or credentials yet obeyed Jing's orders—they ought to suffer manifest execution.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An then impeached Dou Jing for illegally calling out frontier troops, throwing officials and commoners into panic, and for commandery governors who accepted his orders without imperial tallies—conduct that merited public execution.

sentences.zh: 又奏司隸校尉﹑河南尹阿附貴戚，無盡節之義，[一]請免官案罪。

translations.literal: He also memorialized that the metropolitan commandant and the governor of Henan fawned on the exalted kin and lacked the duty to exhaust their loyalty; [1] he asked that they be dismissed and prosecuted.
translations.idiomatic: He further charged the metropolitan commandant and the governor of Henan with toadying to the Dou clan and failing in loyal service; [1] he asked that they be removed and tried.

sentences.zh: 並寢不報。

translations.literal: All were shelved and not answered.
translations.idiomatic: Every memorial was left on the shelf without reply.

sentences.zh: 憲﹑景等日益橫，盡樹其親黨賓客於名都大郡，[二]皆賦斂吏人，更相賂遺，其餘州郡，亦復望風從之。

translations.literal: Xian and Jing daily became more overbearing; they entirely planted their kinsmen, partisans, and retainers in famous commanderies and great prefectures; [2] all levied taxes on clerks and people and exchanged bribes; the other commanderies and prefectures likewise looked to the wind and followed them.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian and Dou Jing grew bolder by the day, packing the great commanderies with their relatives, clients, and guests; [2] everywhere they extorted officials and commoners and traded favors, and other provinces took their cue and did the same.

sentences.zh: 安與任隗舉奏諸二千石，又它所連及貶秩免官者四十餘人，竇氏大恨。

translations.literal: An with Ren Kui jointly memorialized against several two-thousand-picul officials, and those elsewhere implicated who were degraded in rank or dismissed from office numbered more than forty persons; the Dou clan greatly hated them.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An and Ren Kui jointly impeached dozens of senior officials and more than forty others tied to the case who were demoted or dismissed; the Dou family hated them bitterly.

sentences.zh: 但安﹑隗素行高，亦未有以害之。

translations.literal: But An and Kui in their ordinary conduct were lofty, and there was also no means by which to harm them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Yuan An and Ren Kui enjoyed unimpeachable reputations, so the Dous had no lever against them.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢書曰，安奏司隸鄭據﹑河南尹蔡嵩。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: The Continued Han Book says An memorialized against metropolitan commandant Zheng Ju and governor of Henan Cai Song.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Continued Han Book records that Yuan An impeached metropolitan commandant Zheng Ju and Henan governor Cai Song.

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁山松書曰，河南尹王調，漢陽太守朱敞，南陽太守滿殷﹑高丹等皆其賓客。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Yuan Songshu's book says the governor of Henan Wang Tiao, grand administrator of Hanyang Zhu Chang, grand administrators of Nanyang Man Yin and Gao Dan, and others were all their retainers.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Yuan Songshu lists Henan governor Wang Tiao, Hanyang grand administrator Zhu Chang, and Nanyang grand administrators Man Yin and Gao Dan among their clients.

sentences.zh: 前書曰「十二萬戶為大郡」也。

translations.literal: The earlier history says "one hundred twenty thousand households is a great commandery."
translations.idiomatic: The Former Han history defines a "great commandery" as one with a hundred and twenty thousand households.

sentences.zh: 時竇憲復出屯武威。

translations.literal: At that time Dou Xian again went out and encamped at Wuwei.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian had again taken the field and camped at Wuwei.

sentences.zh: 明年，北單于為耿夔所破，遁走烏孫，塞北地空，余部不知所屬。

translations.literal: The next year the northern shanyu was broken by Geng Kui and fled to Wusun; the land north of the passes was empty, and the remaining divisions did not know whom to follow.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Geng Kui shattered the northern shanyu, who fled to Wusun; the steppe north of the passes lay empty and the surviving bands had no leader.

sentences.zh: 憲日矜己功，欲結恩北虜，乃上立降者左鹿蠡王阿佟[一]為北單于，置中郎將領護，如南單于故事。

translations.literal: Xian daily prided himself on his merit and wished to win favor with the northern barbarians; therefore he memorialized to establish the surrendered Left Luoli prince Atong [1] as northern shanyu, placing a chief commandant of household cavalry to oversee and protect him, after the precedent of the southern shanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian grew vain of his victory and sought to bind the northerners to him; he proposed enthroning the surrendered Left Luoli king Atong [1] as northern shanyu under a chief commandant of household cavalry, on the model of the southern court.

sentences.zh: 事下公卿議，太尉宋由﹑太常丁鴻﹑光祿勳耿秉等十人議可許。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down for the dukes and ministers to deliberate; grand commandant Song You, minister of the imperial clan Ding Hong, supervisor of the household Geng Bing, and ten others debated that it could be granted.
translations.idiomatic: The court referred the plan to the ministers; grand commandant Song You, minister of the imperial clan Ding Hong, supervisor of the household Geng Bing, and eight others favored approval.

sentences.zh: 安與任隗奏，以為「光武招懷南虜，非謂可永安內地，正以權時之筭，可得扞御北狄故也。

translations.literal: An with Ren Kui memorialized, considering that "Emperor Guangwu's reason for summoning and cherishing the southern barbarians was not that they could forever secure the inner lands, but because it was a calculation for the moment and could thereby defend against the northern Di.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An and Ren Kui argued that "Emperor Guangwu courted the southern court not because they could permanently pacify the interior, but as a temporary expedient to hold the northern tribes in check.

sentences.zh: 今朔漠既定，宜令南單于反其北庭，並領降觿，無緣復更立阿佟，以增國費」。

translations.literal: Now the northern desert is already settled; it is fitting to order the southern shanyu to return to his northern court and together oversee the surrendered host; there is no reason again to establish Atong and thereby increase state expense."
translations.idiomatic: Now that the northern steppe is quiet, the southern shanyu should be sent back to his old seat to absorb the surrendered bands; enthroning Atong again would only waste the treasury for no gain."

sentences.zh: 宗正劉方﹑大司農尹睦同安議。

translations.literal: Director of the imperial clan Liu Fang and grand minister of agriculture Yin Mu agreed with An's opinion.
translations.idiomatic: Director of the imperial clan Liu Fang and grand minister of agriculture Yin Mu sided with Yuan An.

sentences.zh: 事奏，未以時定。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted it was not settled in timely fashion.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial went in, but the court delayed a decision.

sentences.zh: 安懼憲計遂行，乃獨上封事曰：

translations.literal: An feared Xian's plan would in the end be carried out; therefore alone he submitted a sealed memorial, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Fearing Dou Xian's scheme would go through, Yuan An filed a confidential memorial alone. It read:

sentences.zh: 臣聞功有難圖，不可豫見；

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that merit is hard to plan and cannot be foreseen in advance;
translations.idiomatic: Your servant has heard that great deeds are hard to design and cannot be mapped out beforehand;

sentences.zh: 事有易斷，較然不疑。

translations.literal: matters there are easy to decide on, clear and without doubt.
translations.idiomatic: yet some courses are easy to judge—they stand out plainly and admit no doubt.

sentences.zh: 伏惟光武皇帝本所以立南單于者，欲安南定北之策也，恩德甚備，故匈奴遂分，邊境無患。

translations.literal: I humbly consider that Emperor Guangwu's fundamental reason for establishing the southern shanyu was the policy of securing the south and settling the north; favor and virtue were very complete; therefore the Xiongnu split apart and the border had no trouble.
translations.idiomatic: I submit that Emperor Guangwu founded the southern court as a strategy to stabilize both south and north; his grace was lavish, the Xiongnu divided, and the frontier knew peace.

sentences.zh: 孝明皇帝奉承先意，不敢失墜，赫然命將，爰伐塞北。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoming continued the prior intent and did not dare to let it fall; awesomely he ordered generals and thereupon campaigned north of the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming carried forward that design without slackening; he sent generals in force against the steppe north of the frontier.

sentences.zh: 至乎章和之初，降者十餘萬人，議者欲置之濱塞，東至遼東，[二]太尉宋由﹑光祿勳耿秉皆以為失南單于心，不可，先帝從之。

translations.literal: Down to the beginning of Zhanghe, those who surrendered were more than a hundred thousand persons; debaters wished to place them along the near border, east to Liaodong; [2] grand commandant Song You and supervisor of the household Geng Bing both thought it would lose the southern shanyu's heart and could not be done; the former emperors followed this.
translations.idiomatic: By early Zhanghe over a hundred thousand tribesmen had submitted; some advisers wanted them settled along the inner frontier as far east as Liaodong; [2] grand commandant Song You and supervisor Geng Bing warned that this would alienate the southern shanyu—the late emperors heeded them.

sentences.zh: 陛下奉承洪業，大開疆宇，大將軍遠師討伐，席捲北庭，此誠宣明祖宗，崇立弘勳者也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty continues the great enterprise and greatly opens territory and domain; the great general with a distant army campaigned and struck; rolling up the northern court, this truly is to make clear the ancestors and exalt and establish great merit.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty inherits a mighty enterprise and has widened the realm; the great general's distant campaign swept the northern court—truly a deed that honors the ancestors and raises up lasting glory.

sentences.zh: 宜審其終，以成厥初。

translations.literal: It is fitting to examine its end in order to complete its beginning.
translations.idiomatic: You should weigh the outcome carefully so the beginning may be brought to a worthy close.

sentences.zh: 伏念南單于屯，先父舉觿歸德，自蒙恩以來，四十餘年。

translations.literal: I humbly think of the southern shanyu Tun; his forefathers led the host in turning to virtue; from receiving favor until now more than forty years have passed.
translations.idiomatic: Consider the southern shanyu Tun: his fathers led their people to allegiance; forty years have passed since the court first showed them favor.

sentences.zh: 三帝積累，以遺陛下。

translations.literal: Three emperors accumulated it and bequeathed it to Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Three reigns built that trust and handed it on to you.

sentences.zh: 陛下深宜遵述先志，成就其業。

translations.literal: Your Majesty deeply ought to follow and narrate the prior intent and complete their enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: You ought in all seriousness to follow the intent of your predecessors and bring their policy to fruition.

sentences.zh: 況屯首唱大謀，空盡北虜，輟而弗圖，更立新降，以一朝之計，違三世之規，失信於所養，建立於無功。

translations.literal: Moreover Tun first raised the great plan and emptied the northern barbarians; to stop and not plan further, again to establish a fresh surrender, by a plan of one morning violates the pattern of three generations, loses faith with those you nurture, and establishes one without merit.
translations.idiomatic: Tun himself opened the grand strategy that cleared the north; to abandon that design now, enthrone a new puppet, settle a morning's whim at the cost of three reigns' policy, break faith with an ally you raised up, and elevate a man without merit—

sentences.zh: 由、秉實知舊議，而欲背□先恩。

translations.literal: Song You and Geng Bing truly knew the old discussion yet wish to turn their back □ on prior grace.
translations.idiomatic: Song You and Geng Bing understood the earlier debate yet would abandon the favor shown by past reigns.

sentences.zh: 夫言行君子之樞機，[三]賞罰理國之綱紀。

translations.literal: Speech and conduct are the hinge of the gentleman; [3] reward and punishment are the cords and net of ordering the state.
translations.idiomatic: Word and deed are the pivot of the noble man; [3] reward and punishment are the warp and woof of government.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：『言忠信，行篤敬，雖蠻貊行焉。

translations.literal: The Analects says: "If speech is loyal and trustworthy and conduct is earnest and respectful, even among the Man and Mo one may go."
translations.idiomatic: The Analects says, "Speak with good faith and act with earnest respect, and you may walk even among barbarians."

sentences.zh: 』今若失信於一屯，則百蠻不敢復保誓矣。

translations.literal: " Now if we lose faith with one Tun, then the hundred barbarians will not again dare to keep oaths.
translations.idiomatic: " If we break faith with Tun alone, no frontier people will ever again trust a solemn pledge.

sentences.zh: 又烏桓、鮮卑新殺北單于，凡人之情，鹹畏仇讎，今立其弟，則二虜懷怨。

translations.literal: Moreover the Wuhuan and Xianbei have newly killed the northern shanyu; in the feeling of common men all fear enemies and foes; now if we establish his younger brother, then the two tribes will harbor resentment.
translations.idiomatic: The Wuhuan and Xianbei have just killed the northern shanyu; ordinary men shrink from blood-feud; if we enthrone his younger brother, both peoples will nurse a grudge.

sentences.zh: 兵、食可廢，信不可去。

translations.literal: Troops and food may be dispensed with; trust cannot be cast away.
translations.idiomatic: Arms and grain can be sacrificed; good faith must never be abandoned.

sentences.zh: [四]且漢故事，供給南單于費直歲一億九十餘萬，西域歲七千四百八十萬。

translations.literal: [4] Moreover by Han precedent the yearly cost of supplying the southern shanyu was more than 190 million cash; the Western Regions cost 74.8 million a year.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Under Han precedent the annual outlay for the southern court exceeded a hundred and ninety million cash, and another seventy-four million eight hundred thousand went to the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 今北庭彌遠，其費過倍，是乃空盡天下，而非建策之要也。

translations.literal: Now the northern court is still farther; its expense exceeds double; this is to empty the whole realm and is not the crux of sound policy.
translations.idiomatic: The new northern court would lie even farther away and cost more than twice as much—an effort to drain the empire for no strategic gain.

sentences.zh: 詔下其議。

translations.literal: An edict sent the matter down for discussion.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ordered the proposal circulated for debate.

sentences.zh: 安又與憲更相難折。

translations.literal: An again with Xian mutually refuted and overbore each other.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An and Dou Xian traded bitter rebuttals in court.

sentences.zh: 憲險急負埶，言辭驕訐，[五]至詆毀安，稱光武誅韓歆、戴涉故事，安終不移。

translations.literal: Xian was perilous and pressing and relied on his position; his language was arrogant and fault-finding; [5] he went so far as to slander An, citing the precedent of Emperor Guangwu executing Han Xi and Dai She—An in the end did not yield.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian was sharp, overbearing, and drunk on his power; his tone turned arrogant and abusive; [5] he even smeared Yuan An by invoking Guangwu's executions of Han Xi and Dai She—yet Yuan An never budged.

sentences.zh: [六]憲竟立匈奴降者右鹿蠡王於除鞬為單于，[七]後遂反叛，卒如安策。

translations.literal: [6] Xian in the end established the surrendered Xiongnu right Luoli king Yuchujian as shanyu; [7] afterward they rebelled, and in the end it was as An had predicted.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Dou Xian still enthroned the surrendered Xiongnu prince Yuchujian of the Right Luoli band as shanyu; [7] the northerners soon revolted, exactly as Yuan An had warned.

sentences.zh: 注[一]徒冬反。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Fanqie reading: initial from the syllable spelled here as 徒, final from 冬.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The commentary gives the fanqie spelling for the character in question.

sentences.zh: 注[二]濱，邊也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: 滨 means "border."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Here the word glossed means the littoral or frontier strip.

sentences.zh: 注[三]易曰：「言行者，君子之樞機。

translations.literal: Comment [3] The Changes says: "Speech and conduct are the gentleman's pivot.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The Book of Changes reads, "Word and deed are the hinge of the noble man.

sentences.zh: 樞機之發，榮辱之主也。」

translations.literal: When the pivot moves, it is lord of glory and disgrace."
translations.idiomatic: When that hinge turns, it governs honor and shame."

sentences.zh: 注[四]論語：「孔子曰：『足食足兵，人信之矣。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Analects: "Confucius said, 'With ample food, ample troops, the people will have trust.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Analects records Confucius saying, "Give the people enough to eat, enough soldiers, and they will trust the ruler."

sentences.zh: 』『必不得已而去，於斯三者何先？

translations.literal: 'If one must abandon one of these three, which first?'
translations.idiomatic: "If you were forced to give up one of the three, which would go first?"

sentences.zh: 』曰：『去兵。

translations.literal: He said, 'Abandon the troops.'"
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "Strip away the army."

sentences.zh: 』曰：『必不得已而去，於斯二者何先？

translations.literal: 'He said, 'If one must abandon one of these two, which first?'
translations.idiomatic: "If you still had to surrender one of the two, which would it be?"

sentences.zh: 』曰：『去食。

translations.literal: He said, 'Abandon food.'"
translations.idiomatic: "Let go of food."

sentences.zh: 自古皆有死，人無信不立。』」

translations.literal: From antiquity there has been death; without trust the people cannot stand.'"
translations.idiomatic: "Death has always been with us, but a state cannot stand if its people do not trust it."

sentences.zh: 注[五]訐謂發揚人之惡。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: 訐 means to expose and spread others' evil.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: Here the gloss explains the word as "to air another's faults in public."

sentences.zh: 注[六]大司徒歆坐非帝讀隗囂書，自殺。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: Grand minister of education Xi was sentenced because he was not yet emperor but read Wei Xiao's letter; he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: Han Xi, grand minister of education, was charged with reading Wei Xiao's correspondence while still a commoner and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 大司徒涉坐殺太倉令，下獄死。

translations.literal: Grand minister of education She was sentenced for killing the director of the grand granary; he died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6 continued]: Dai She, likewise grand minister of education, was condemned for executing the granary intendant and died in jail.

sentences.zh: 注[七]鞬音九言反。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: 鞬 is read with fanqie initial 九 and final 言.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7]: The commentary supplies the fanqie spelling for the second syllable of the Xiongnu prince's name.

sentences.zh: 安以天子幼弱，外戚□權，每朝會進見，及與公卿言國家事，未嘗不噫嗚流涕。

translations.literal: An because the Son of Heaven was young and weak and the consort kin □ held power, whenever at court assembly he advanced to audience or discussed state affairs with the dukes and ministers, he never failed to sigh and weep.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was a boy and the maternal kin monopolized power; whenever Yuan An came to court or debated policy with the high ministers, he would end in audible sobs.

sentences.zh: [一]自天子及大臣皆恃賴之。

translations.literal: [1] From the Son of Heaven down to great ministers all depended on him.
translations.idiomatic: [1] From the throne to the senior ministers, everyone leaned on him.

sentences.zh: 四年春，薨，朝廷痛惜焉。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, spring, he died; the court grieved for him.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the spring of the fourth year of the reign; the whole bureaucracy mourned him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]噫音醫，又乙戒反。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: 噫 is read 醫, also with fanqie 乙 戒.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The sigh-word is glossed with the sound yi and an alternate fanqie reading.

sentences.zh: 嗚音一故反。

translations.literal: 呜 is read with fanqie 一 故.
translations.idiomatic: The character for the wail is glossed with another fanqie pair.

sentences.zh: 歎傷之貌也。

translations.literal: The appearance of grieving in sorrow.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary defines the compound as the look and sound of grief-stricken weeping.

sentences.zh: 初，安父沒，母使安訪求葬地，道逢三書生，問安何之，安為言其故，生乃指一處，云「葬此地，當世為上公」。

translations.literal: At first when An's father died, his mother sent An to seek a burial site; on the road he met three scholars who asked An where he was going; An told them the reason; the scholars then pointed to a place, saying "Bury here and for generations you will be supreme dukes."
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan An's father died, his mother sent him to find a grave site. On the way he met three scholars who asked his errand; when he explained, they pointed to a plot and said, "Inter your father here and your line will produce grandees for generations."

sentences.zh: 須臾不見，安異之。

translations.literal: In a moment they were not seen; An thought it strange.
translations.idiomatic: They vanished in an instant; Yuan An knew something uncanny had happened.

sentences.zh: 於是遂葬其所佔之地，故累世隆盛焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon he buried at the place they had indicated; therefore for successive generations they flourished greatly.
translations.idiomatic: He buried his father on the spot they named, and the Yuan clan rose to extraordinary eminence thereafter.

sentences.zh: 安子京、敞最知名。

translations.literal: An's sons Jing and Chang were most renowned.
translations.idiomatic: Among his sons Yuan Jing and Yuan Chang became the most celebrated.

sentences.zh: 京字仲譽。

translations.literal: Jing's courtesy name was Zhongyu.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yuan Jing bore the courtesy name Zhongyu.

sentences.zh: 習孟氏易，作難記三十萬言。

translations.literal: He studied the Meng school of the Changes and composed hard notes in three hundred thousand characters.
translations.idiomatic: He studied the Meng tradition of the Book of Changes and wrote three hundred thousand characters of critical notes.

sentences.zh: 初拜郎中，稍遷侍中，出為蜀郡太守。

translations.literal: At first he was appointed gentleman of the palace and gradually rose to attendant at the palace and went out as grand administrator of Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He began as a gentleman of the palace, rose to palace attendant, and was then posted grand administrator of Shu.

sentences.zh: 子彭，字伯楚。

translations.literal: His son Peng, courtesy name Bochu.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yuan Peng was known by the courtesy name Bochu.

sentences.zh: 少傳父業，歷廣漢、南陽太守。

translations.literal: In youth he transmitted his father's occupation and successively served as grand administrator of Guanghan and Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: He inherited his father's scholarship and served in turn as grand administrator of Guanghan and Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 順帝初，為光禒勳。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Shundi's reign he became supervisor of the household (Guangluxun).
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Shun ascended the throne, he was appointed supervisor of the household.

sentences.zh: 行至清，為吏麤袍糲食，終於議郎。

translations.literal: His conduct was extremely pure; as an official he wore coarse robes and ate coarse food and ended as gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: He was austere almost to a fault, wearing homespun and eating plain fare, and died in the humble rank of gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 尚書胡廣等追表其有清絜之美，比前朝貢禹、第五倫。

translations.literal: Supervisor of the masters Hu Guang and others jointly memorialized that he had the beauty of purity and incorruptibility, comparable to Gong Yu and the Fifth Lun of the former court.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Guang of the masters office and others petitioned together, praising his spotless integrity and comparing him to Gong Yu and Fifth Lun of the Former Han.

sentences.zh: [一]未蒙顯贈，當時皆嗟歎之。

translations.literal: [1] He did not receive a visible posthumous enfeoffment; the age all sighed over it.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The court never granted him a fitting posthumous honor, and his contemporaries lamented it.

sentences.zh: 注[一]貢禹，元帝御史大夫。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Gong Yu was imperial counselor under Emperor Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Gong Yu had risen to imperial counselor under Emperor Yuan of the Former Han.

sentences.zh: 經明行修，清絜憂國也。

translations.literal: Clear in classics, cultivated in conduct, pure and anxious for the state.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss praises men learned in the classics, upright in life, frugal, and devoted to the public good.

sentences.zh: 彭弟湯

translations.literal: Peng's younger brother Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Tang, Yuan Peng's younger brother.

sentences.zh: 彭弟湯，字仲河，少傳家學，諸儒稱其節，多歷顯位。

translations.literal: Peng's younger brother Tang, courtesy name Zhonghe, in youth transmitted the family learning; the scholars praised his integrity; he repeatedly held high office.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Tang, courtesy Zhonghe, Yuan Peng's brother, continued the family scholarship; Confucian circles admired his moral fiber, and he rose through a series of eminent posts.

sentences.zh: 桓帝初為司空，以豫議定策封安國亭侯，食邑五百戶。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Huandi's reign he was minister of works; for participating in the council that fixed the succession he was enfeoffed as marquis of Anguo village, five hundred households of revenue.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Huan's reign he became minister of works; for advising on the choice of heir he was made village marquis of Anguo with a fief of five hundred households.

sentences.zh: 累遷司徒、太尉，以□異策免。

translations.literal: Cumulative promotion to minister of education and grand commandant; because of □ portent policy he was dismissed by edict.
translations.idiomatic: He climbed to minister of education and then grand commandant until anomalous omens linked to his policies brought his dismissal by edict.

sentences.zh: 卒，謚曰康侯。

translations.literal: He died; posthumous title was Marquis Kang.
translations.idiomatic: He died and received the posthumous title Marquis Kang.

sentences.zh: 〈《風俗通》曰：「湯時年八十六，有子十二人。」〉

translations.literal: Customs and Usages says: "When Tang was eighty-six he had twelve sons."
translations.idiomatic: The Customs and Usages records that at eighty-six Yuan Tang fathered twelve sons.

sentences.zh: 湯長子成，左中郎*[將]*。

translations.literal: Tang's eldest son Cheng was left gentleman, chief commandant *[of cavalry]*.
translations.idiomatic: His eldest son Yuan Cheng served as chief commandant on the left among the gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 早卒，次子逢嗣。

translations.literal: He died early; the second son Feng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Cheng died young, and the marquisate passed to the second son, Yuan Feng.

sentences.zh: 湯子逢

translations.literal: Feng, son of Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Feng, son of Yuan Tang.

sentences.zh: 逢字周陽，以累世三公子，寬厚篤信，著稱於時。

translations.literal: Feng's courtesy name was Zhouyang; because for accumulated generations he was a house of thrice-made dukes, he was generous, sincere, and trustworthy and was renowned in the age.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Feng, courtesy Zhouyang, came from a line that had thrice reached the three dukedoms; his openhanded loyalty won him wide renown.

sentences.zh: 靈帝立，逢以太僕豫議，增封三百戶。

translations.literal: When Lingdi was established, Feng for grand coachman participated in the council and his fief was increased three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ling ascended the throne, Yuan Feng took part in the deliberations as grand coachman and gained another three hundred households on his fief.

sentences.zh: 後為司空，卒於執金吾。

translations.literal: Later he was minister of works and died in the office of bearer of the golden mace.
translations.idiomatic: He later served as minister of works and died while holding the post of bearer of the golden mace.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以逢嘗為三老，特優禮之，賜以珠畫特詔秘器，[一]飯含珠玉二十六品，[二]使五官中郎將持節奉策，贈以車騎將軍印綬，加號特進，謚曰宣文侯。

translations.literal: The court because Feng had once been one of the Three Elders especially treated him with generous rites, granting him pearl-decorated special-edict secret burial goods, [1] twenty-six grades of mouth-gems and jade for burial, [2] ordered the chief commandant of gentlemen for the household to bear credentials and present the patent, posthumously granted the seals and ribbons of chariots and cavalry general, added title specially advanced, posthumous title Marquis Xuanwen.
translations.idiomatic: Because Yuan Feng had once been honored as a Three Elder, the court buried him with extraordinary ceremony: a vermilion-painted coffin provided by special edict, [1] twenty-six grades of mortuary jade for the mouth, [2] a chief commandant of household gentlemen bearing the imperial baton to read the patent, posthumous appointment as chariot-and-cavalry general with the added dignity "specially advanced," and the posthumous name Marquis Xuanwen.

sentences.zh: 子基嗣，位至太僕。

translations.literal: His son Ji succeeded; he reached the office of grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yuan Ji inherited the title and rose to grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《前書》曰，董賢死，以沙畫棺。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Former Documents says when Dong Xian died they used a sand-painted coffin.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Former Han history records that Dong Xian's coffin was painted with cinnabar dust.

sentences.zh: 音義云：「以朱沙畫之也。

translations.literal: Yin-yi says: "With vermillion sand they painted it."
translations.idiomatic: The sound gloss explains that the surface was painted with red sand.

sentences.zh: 」「珠」與「朱」同。

translations.literal: "Pearl" here is the same sound as "vermillion."
translations.idiomatic: The word translated "pearl" here puns on the word for vermillion.

sentences.zh: 秘器，棺也。

translations.literal: Secret goods means a coffin.
translations.idiomatic: "Secret burial goods" means the inner coffin set.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《谷梁傳》曰：「貝玉曰含。」

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Guliang commentary says: "Beads and jade in the mouth is called han."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Guliang commentary defines mortuary jade placed in the mouth.

sentences.zh: 逢弟隗

translations.literal: Younger brother of Feng, Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Wei, younger brother of Yuan Feng.

sentences.zh: 逢弟隗，少歷顯官，

translations.literal: Younger brother of Feng Wei, in youth he passed through illustrious offices,
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Wei, Feng's younger brother, rose early through prominent posts,

sentences.zh: 〈隗字次陽。〉

translations.literal: <Wei's courtesy name was Ciyang.>
translations.idiomatic: bearing the courtesy name Ciyang.

sentences.zh: 先逢為三公。

translations.literal: Before Feng he became one of the Three Dukes.
translations.idiomatic: He entered the three dukedoms even before Yuan Feng did.

sentences.zh: 時中常侍袁赦，隗之宗也，用事於中。

translations.literal: At that time palace attendant Yuan She, of the same clan as Wei, wielded power at court.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuch Yuan She, a kinsman, dominated the inner court at the time.

sentences.zh: 以逢、隗世宰相家，推崇以為外援。

translations.literal: Because Feng and Wei were of a house of hereditary chancellors, they honored and promoted them as external support.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuans treated Feng and Wei as pillars of a ministerial dynasty and leaned on them as allies outside the palace.

sentences.zh: 故袁氏貴寵於世，富奢甚，不與它公族同。

translations.literal: Therefore the Yuan clan was noble and favored in the age, extremely rich and extravagant, not the same as other ducal houses.
translations.idiomatic: The clan therefore enjoyed unmatched favor, fabulous wealth, and a swagger no other great family could match.

sentences.zh: 獻帝初，隗為太傳。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Xiandi, Wei was grand tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Xian's reign Yuan Wei became grand tutor.

sentences.zh: 成子紹，逢子術，自有傳。

translations.literal: Cheng's son Shao and Feng's son Shu have their own biographies.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao, son of Yuan Cheng, and Yuan Shu, son of Yuan Feng, receive full biographies elsewhere.

sentences.zh: 董卓忿紹、術背己，遂誅隗及術兄基男女二十餘人。

translations.literal: Dong Zhuo resented Shao and Shu for turning against him, therefore executed Wei and Shu's elder brother Ji—men and women more than twenty persons.
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan Shao and Yuan Shu broke with Dong Zhuo, the warlord slaughtered Yuan Wei and Yuan Ji—Yuan Shu's elder brother—along with more than twenty men and women of the family.

sentences.zh: 京弟敞

translations.literal: Younger brother of Jing, Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Chang, younger brother of Yuan Jing.

sentences.zh: 敞字叔平，少傳易經教授，以父任為太子舍人。

translations.literal: Chang's courtesy name was Shuping; in youth he transmitted the Book of Changes as classic teacher; through his father's position he became gentleman attendant at the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Chang, courtesy Shuping, taught the Book of Changes while young and entered office as crown prince's gentleman by his father's privilege.

sentences.zh: 和帝時，歷位將軍、大夫、侍中，出為東郡太守，征拜太僕、光祿勳。

translations.literal: In He Emperor's time he successively held posts as general, grandee, attendant at the palace; went out as grand administrator of Dong commandery; was summoned and appointed grand coachman and supervisor of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor He he served as general, grandee, and palace attendant, governed Dong commandery, and was recalled to be grand coachman and supervisor of the household.

sentences.zh: 元初三年，代劉愷為司空。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanchu he replaced Liu Kai as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu 3 he succeeded Liu Kai as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 明年，坐子與尚書郎張俊交通，漏洩省中語，策免。

translations.literal: The next year for his son's communication with secretary Zhang Jun and leaking secret language from the privy chamber he was dismissed by edict.
translations.idiomatic: The next year his son's dealings with secretary Zhang Jun and the leak of palace secrets cost him his post by edict.

sentences.zh: 敞廉勁不阿權貴，失鄧氏旨，遂自殺。

translations.literal: Chang was incorrupt and stern and did not curry great families; he missed the Deng clan's intent and thereupon killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Chang was upright and unbending and would not truckle to great clans; he crossed the Deng family and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 張俊者，蜀郡人，有才能，與兄龕並為尚書郎，年少勵鋒氣。

translations.literal: Zhang Jun was a man of Shu commandery, had talent and ability; together with his elder brother Kan he was secretary; young in years, keen in edge and spirit.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jun of Shu was talented; he and his brother Zhang Kan both served as secretaries, young men full of fire.

sentences.zh: 郎朱濟、丁盛立行不修，俊欲舉奏之，二人聞，恐，因郎陳重、雷義往請俊，俊不聽，因共私賂侍史，使求俊短，得其私書與敞子，遂封上之，皆下獄，當死。

translations.literal: The secretaries Zhu Ji and Ding Sheng did not cultivate their conduct; Jun wished to memorialize against them; the two heard and were afraid, and through the secretaries Chen Chong and Lei Yi went to ask Jun; Jun would not listen; therefore together they privately bribed the head clerk, had him seek Jun's faults, obtained his private letter to Chang's son; thereupon sealed it and sent it up—all were sent to prison and condemned to death.
translations.idiomatic: Fellow secretaries Zhu Ji and Ding Sheng lived loosely; Zhang Jun meant to impeach them. They panicked, sent Chen Chong and Lei Yi to plead, and when Zhang Jun refused they bribed the chief clerk to dig up dirt, seized a private letter he had written to Yuan Chang's son, sealed it, and forwarded it to the throne. Everyone named in the case landed in prison under sentence of death.

sentences.zh: 俊自獄中占獄吏上書自訟，[一]書奏而俊獄已報。

translations.literal: From prison Jun dictated to the jail clerk a memorial to clear himself, [1] when the memorial was presented Jun's case had already been reported (for execution).
translations.idiomatic: From his cell Zhang Jun dictated a self-defense memorial to the jailer, [1] but by the time it reached the throne his sentence had already been confirmed.

sentences.zh: [二]廷尉將出谷門，臨行刑，[三]鄧太后詔馳騎以減死論。

translations.literal: [2] The commandant of justice was about to exit Gumen and proceed to execution; [3] Empress Dowager Deng's edict sent mounted courier with commutation to spared death.
translations.idiomatic: [2] As the commandant of justice led him out through the Gumen gate for execution, [3] Empress Dowager Deng's rescript raced in on horseback and commuted the penalty to life.

sentences.zh: 俊假名上書謝曰：「臣孤恩負義，自陷重刑，情斷意訖，無所復望。

translations.literal: Jun under a false name submitted a memorial thanking: "Your servant alone has betrayed grace and violated duty, plunged himself into heavy punishment; my feeling is cut off and intent ended; I have nothing more to hope."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jun, writing under an alias, thanked the throne: "I alone betrayed your grace and broke the law; I deserve the extreme penalty and have no further hope."

sentences.zh: 廷尉鞠遣，歐[四]刀在前，棺絮在後，魂魄飛揚，形容已枯。

translations.literal: As the commandant of justice interrogated and released, the executioner's knife before, coffin and wadding behind, my soul flying aloft, my form already withered."
translations.idiomatic: "When the commandant of justice finished his inquest and marched me out, the executioner's blade gleamed ahead and the hearse with its padding waited behind; my soul scattered and my body seemed already a corpse."

sentences.zh: 陛下聖澤，以臣嘗在近密，[五]識其狀貌，傷其眼目，留心曲慮，特加□覆。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's sage flood; because your servant once was near the privy [five], knew his appearance, pitied his eyes, attended with crooked care, especially extended sheltering cover."
translations.idiomatic: "Yet your sacred kindness—because I once served inside the masters' office [five] and you recognized my face—moved you to pity and to bend the law especially in my favor."

sentences.zh: 喪車復還，白骨更肉，披棺發幟，起見白日。

translations.literal: The hearse turned back; white bones again had flesh; tearing open the coffin and lifting the shroud, I rise and see the white sun."
translations.idiomatic: "The death-cart turned round; dry bones grew flesh again; the lid was lifted and the winding-sheet torn away, and I walked once more under the open sun."

sentences.zh: 天地父母能生臣俊，不能使臣俊當死復生。

translations.literal: Heaven and earth and my parents could give birth to your servant Jun but could not make your servant Jun when about to die return to life."
translations.idiomatic: "Heaven and earth and my parents gave me life but could not snatch me back from the grave."

sentences.zh: 陛下德過天地，恩重父母，誠非臣俊破碎骸骨，舉宗腐爛，所報萬一。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's virtue passes heaven and earth; grace is heavier than parents; truly it is not something your servant Jun by smashing bones and powdering skeleton and whole clan rotting can repay in one ten-thousandth."
translations.idiomatic: "Your virtue outshines heaven and earth; your favor outweighs a parent's—not one part in ten thousand could I repay by grinding my bones or offering my whole clan to rot."

sentences.zh: 臣俊徒也，不得上書；

translations.literal: Your servant Jun is a convict and may not submit memorials;"
translations.idiomatic: "As a convict I had no right to memorialize;"

sentences.zh: 不勝去死就生，驚喜踴躍，觸冒拜章。

translations.literal: Unable to bear going from death to life, surprised and joyful, I leap and touch risk to bow with a chapter."
translations.idiomatic: "yet I could not contain my leap from death to life, and in reckless gratitude I dare lay this petition before you."

sentences.zh: 」當時皆哀其文。

translations.literal: " At the time all pitied his text.
translations.idiomatic: " Everyone who read it wept for him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]占謂口授也，前書曰「陳遵憑幾口占書吏」是也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: 占 means dictated orally; the earlier history's "Chen Zun leaning on the table orally dictated for the clerk" is this.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: "Dictated" means spoken for another to write, as when Chen Zun dictated a memorial from his couch in the Earlier Han account.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謂奏報論死也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Means the memorial had been reported sentencing death.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: That is, the return slip from the throne had already fixed the death sentence.

sentences.zh: 注[三]谷門，洛陽城北面中門也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Gumen was the middle gate on the north side of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Gumen was the middle gate in the north wall of Luoyang, where executions were led out.

sentences.zh: 注[四]音一口反。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: read with fanqie 一 口.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The commentary gives the fanqie spelling for the preceding graph.

sentences.zh: 注[五]謂為尚書郎。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: Refers to serving as secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: "Near the privy chamber" refers to Zhang Jun's former post as secretary.

sentences.zh: 朝廷由此薄敞罪而隱其死，以三公禮葬之，復其官。

translations.literal: The court thereby thought lightly of Chang's crime but concealed his death, buried him with three-duke rites, restored his office.
translations.idiomatic: The court therefore softened Yuan Chang's guilt, hushed up the manner of his death, buried him with honors due a three-duke, and restored his titles.

sentences.zh: 子盱。

translations.literal: His son was named Yu.
translations.idiomatic: He left a son, Yuan Yu, who carried on the line.
Editorial note [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]況於反。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: read with fanqie 况 于.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The commentary gives the fanqie reading for the personal name.

sentences.zh: 盱後至光祿勳。

translations.literal: Yu later reached supervisor of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Yu later rose to supervisor of the household.

sentences.zh: 時大將軍梁冀□朝，內外莫不阿附，唯盱與廷尉邯鄲義正身自守。

translations.literal: At that time great general Liang Ji dominated the court; inside and outside none did not curry favor; only Yu together with commandant of justice Yi of Handan straightened the body and guarded themselves.
translations.idiomatic: While Grand General Liang Ji ran the court, the whole bureaucracy toadied to him; only Yuan Yu and Yi of Handan, the commandant of justice, kept their integrity.

sentences.zh: 及桓帝誅冀，使盱持節收其印綬，事已具梁冀傳。

translations.literal: When Huandi executed Ji, he ordered Yu to bear credentials and seize his seals and ribbons; the affair is fully in Liang Ji's biography.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Huan destroyed Liang Ji, he sent Yuan Yu with the imperial baton to confiscate his seals; the full story is told in Liang Ji's memoir.

sentences.zh: 彭孫閎

translations.literal: Peng's grandson Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong, grandson of Yuan Peng.

sentences.zh: 閎字夏甫，彭之孫也。

translations.literal: Hong's courtesy name was Xiafu; he was Peng's grandson.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong, style Xiafu, was a grandson of Yuan Peng.

sentences.zh: 少勵操行，苦身修節。

translations.literal: In youth he urged on conduct and toiled the body to cultivate integrity.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he hardened his character and disciplined himself to live austerely.

sentences.zh: 父賀，為彭城相。

translations.literal: His father He was chancellor of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: His father Yuan He served as chancellor to the king of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: [一]閎往省謁，變名姓，徒行無旅。

translations.literal: [1] Hong went to visit and pay respects, changed his name, walked on foot and had no retinue.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When he called on his father he traveled under an alias, on foot and without attendants.

sentences.zh: 既至府門，連日吏不為通，會阿母出，見閎驚，[二]入白夫人，乃密呼見。

translations.literal: When he reached the headquarters gate, for successive days the clerks would not announce him; it happened that the wet nurse came out, saw Hong and was startled; [2] she entered and told the lady; then secretly called him in to audience.
translations.idiomatic: For days the gate clerks refused him entry until the wet nurse stepped out, recognized him with a start, [2] and reported to Lady Yuan, who had him brought in secretly.

sentences.zh: 既而辭去，賀遣車送之，閎稱眩疾不肯乘，反，郡界無知者。

translations.literal: Presently he took leave to depart; He sent a carriage to see him off; Hong claimed dizzy illness and would not ride; returning, none in the commandery knew.
translations.idiomatic: When he left, Yuan He sent a cart; Yuan Hong pleaded vertigo and walked home, so nobody in the commandery realized who he was.

sentences.zh: 及賀卒郡，閎兄弟迎喪，不受賻贈，縗絰扶柩，冒犯寒露，禮貌枯毀，手足血流，見者莫不傷之。

translations.literal: When He died in the commandery, Hong and his brothers went to meet the coffin; they did not accept condolence gifts; in mourning headband and cord they supported the bier, braving cold dew; their ritual bearing wasted their bodies; hands and feet flowed blood; those who saw them all pitied them.
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan He died in office, Yuan Hong and his brothers fetched the hearse, refused funeral gifts, and in full mourning hauled the coffin through bitter weather until their hands bled; every onlooker was moved.

sentences.zh: 服闋，累徵聘舉召，皆不應。

translations.literal: When mourning ended, accumulated summons by recruitment and recommendation and summons, all he did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: After the mourning period ended, repeated nominations and imperial calls never drew a response.

sentences.zh: 居處仄陋，以耕學為業。

translations.literal: His dwelling was narrow and mean; he took plowing and study as occupation.
translations.idiomatic: He lived in a cramped hut and earned his keep by farming and reading.

sentences.zh: 從父逢、隗並貴盛，數饋之，無所受。

translations.literal: His uncle by the father's line Feng and Wei were both noble and flourishing; repeatedly they sent gifts; he accepted none.
translations.idiomatic: His cousins Yuan Feng and Yuan Wei were rich and powerful and often sent him money; he turned it all away.

sentences.zh: 注[一]風俗通曰：「賀字符服。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Customs and Usages says: "He's courtesy name was Fufu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Customs and Usages gives Yuan He's style as Fufu.

sentences.zh: 祖父京，為侍中。

translations.literal: Grandfather Jing was attendant at the palace.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Yuan Jing had been a palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 安帝始加元服，百僚會賀，臨莊垂出而孫適生，喜其嘉會，因名字焉。」

translations.literal: When Emperor An first added the cap of manhood, the hundred officials assembled to congratulate; as he was about to leave the solemn hall a grandson happened to be born; rejoicing at the auspicious gathering, therefore he gave the name He."
translations.idiomatic: At Emperor An's coming-of-age rite the court gathered to congratulate; as Yuan He was leaving the hall a grandson was born, and in joy at the happy coincidence he named the child He."

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「乳母從內出，見在門側，面貌省瘦，為其垂泣。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Xie Cheng's book says: "The wet nurse came from within, saw him at the side of the gate, face thin from care, and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Xie Cheng writes that the nurse found him haggard beside the gate and wept.

sentences.zh: 閎厚丁寧：『此閒不知吾，慎勿宣露也。』」

translations.literal: Hong earnestly instructed: 'Here they do not know me; be careful not to disclose.'"
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong urged her, "They must not learn who I am—tell no one."

sentences.zh: 閎見時方險亂，而家門富盛，常對兄弟歎曰：「吾先公福祚，後世不能以德守之，而競為驕奢，與亂世爭權，此即晉之三卻矣。

translations.literal: Hong saw the age was perilous and chaotic while his family gate was rich and flourishing; often to his brothers he sighed, saying: "Our former dukes' blessing and fortune; posterity cannot guard it with virtue, but compete in pride and extravagance, contending for power with a chaotic age—this is the three Xi of Jin."
translations.idiomatic: Seeing a lawless age and a household bloated with wealth, Yuan Hong often told his brothers, "Our ancestors left us fortune we cannot keep by virtue; we vie in luxury and scramble for power in dark times—the house of Jin's three Xi clans all over again."

sentences.zh: 」[一]延熹末，黨事將作，閎遂散發絕世，欲投多深林。

translations.literal: " [1] At the end of Yanxi, when the faction case was about to arise, Hong thereupon loosed hair and cut off from the world, wishing to cast himself into deep forest.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Near the end of the Yanxi era, as the partisan prosecutions loomed, he let his hair hang wild and withdrew from society, ready to flee to the deep woods.

sentences.zh: 以母老不宜遠遁，乃築土室，四周於庭，不為戶，自牖納飲食而已。

translations.literal: Because his mother was old it was not fitting to flee far; therefore he built an earthen chamber, on all four sides within the courtyard, made no door, and only through the window received drink and food.
translations.idiomatic: His mother's age forbade flight, so he raised an earthen cell in the courtyard without a door, taking food only through a window.

sentences.zh: 旦於室中東向拜母。

translations.literal: At dawn within the chamber he bowed east to his mother.
translations.idiomatic: Each dawn he faced east inside the cell and bowed to his mother.

sentences.zh: 母思閎，時往就視，母去，便自掩閉，兄弟妻子莫得見也。

translations.literal: The mother missed Hong; from time to time she went to look; when the mother left, he then himself closed and barred; brothers, wife, and children could not obtain to see him.
translations.idiomatic: She missed him and sometimes peered in; when she left he sealed the window again, and even his brothers and family never saw his face.

sentences.zh: 及母歿，不為制服設位，時莫能名，或以為狂生。

translations.literal: When his mother died, he did not make mourning garments or set up a spirit seat; the age could not name it; some thought him a mad scholar.
translations.idiomatic: When she died he wore no mourning and set up no shrine; people could not fathom his conduct and called him a madman.

sentences.zh: 潛身十八年，黃巾賊起，攻沒郡縣，百姓驚散，閎誦經不移。

translations.literal: He hid his person eighteen years; when Yellow Turban bandits arose, attacking and drowning commanderies and counties, the hundred surnames scattered in alarm; Hong recited the classics and did not move.
translations.idiomatic: He stayed hidden eighteen years; when the Yellow Turbans overran the countryside and the people fled, he kept chanting the classics without stirring.

sentences.zh: 賊相約語不入其閭，卿人就閎避難，皆得全免。

translations.literal: The bandits mutually agreed in speech not to enter his lane; people of the commandery went to Hong to avoid disaster; all obtained complete escape.
translations.idiomatic: The rebels pledged to spare his lane, and neighbors who sheltered with him all survived.

sentences.zh: 年五十七，卒於土室。

translations.literal: At age fifty-seven he died in the earthen chamber.
translations.idiomatic: He died in that cell at fifty-seven.

sentences.zh: [二]二弟忠、弘，節操皆亞於閎。

translations.literal: [2] Two younger brothers Zhong and Hong, integrity and conduct were all next to Hong.
translations.idiomatic: [2] His brothers Yuan Zhong and Yuan Hong ranked just below him in moral stature.

sentences.zh: 注[一]三卻謂卻錡、卻粲、卻至，皆晉卿也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The three Xi means Xi Qi, Xi Can, Xi Zhi, all were ministers of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The "three Xi" were Xi Qi, Xi Can, and Xi Zhi, grandees of Jin who perished through their own arrogance.

sentences.zh: 各驕奢，為厲公所殺。

translations.literal: Each was proud and extravagant and was killed by Duke Li.
translations.idiomatic: Each grew haughty and wasteful and fell to Duke Li's purge.

sentences.zh: 事見左傳。

translations.literal: The affair is seen in Zuo's commentary.
translations.idiomatic: The story is in the Zuo Tradition.

sentences.zh: 注[二]汝南先賢傳曰：「閎臨卒，□其子曰：『勿設殯棺，但著褌衫疏布單衣幅巾，親屍於板默之上，以五百墼為藏。』」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Worthies of Runan says: "When Hong was near death, □ his sons: 'Do not set out mourning coffin, but put on only trousers, thin cloth single garment, and headcloth; lay the corpse on a board above the silent place; use five hundred bricks as burial."'"
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Worthies of Runan records his deathbed words: "No outer coffin—only drawers, a thin gown, and a cloth cap; lay me on a plank above the vault; bury me with five hundred bricks."

sentences.zh: 閎弟忠

translations.literal: Hong's younger brother Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Zhong, younger brother of Yuan Hong.

sentences.zh: 忠字正甫，與同郡范滂為友，俱證黨事得釋，語在

translations.literal: Zhong's courtesy name was Zhengfu; with Fan Pang of the same commandery he was friends; both testified in the faction case and obtained release; the language is in
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Zhong, style Zhengfu, was friends with Fan Pang of their commandery; both were cleared after testifying in the partisan case, as told in

sentences.zh: 〈滂傳〉

translations.literal: Pang's biography
translations.idiomatic: the biography of Fan Pang

translations.literal: Period mark.
translations.idiomatic: The sentence concludes the cross-reference to Fan Pang's biography.

sentences.zh: 初平中，為沛相，[一]乘葦車到官，以清亮稱。

translations.literal: In the middle of Chuping he was chancellor of Pei; [1] riding a reed cart he arrived at office; by pure brightness he was praised.
translations.idiomatic: During the Chuping years he became chancellor of Pei; [1] he took office in a reed-woven cart and won fame for incorruptibility.

sentences.zh: 及天下大亂，忠□官客會稽上虞。

translations.literal: When all under heaven was greatly chaotic, Zhong □ office and sojourned as guest in Shangyu of Kuaiji.
translations.idiomatic: When the realm collapsed into chaos he resigned and lodged as a private guest in Shangyu, Kuaiji.

sentences.zh: [二]一見太守王朗徒從整飾，心嫌之，遂稱病自絕。

translations.literal: [2] One sight of grand administrator Wang Lang's followers in neat array, in heart he disliked it; thereupon claimed illness and cut himself off.
translations.idiomatic: [2] One glimpse of Governor Wang Lang's polished retinue disgusted him; he pleaded illness and broke off contact.

sentences.zh: [三]後孫策破會稽，忠等浮海南投交址。

translations.literal: [3] Later Sun Ce broke Kuaiji; Zhong and others floated on the sea south to throw themselves on Jiaozhi.
translations.idiomatic: [3] After Sun Ce conquered Kuaiji, Yuan Zhong and his companions sailed south to take refuge in Jiaozhi.

sentences.zh: 獻帝都許，征為□尉，未到，卒。

translations.literal: When Xiandi established the capital at Xu, he was summoned as commandant of the guards; before arriving he died.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian summoned him to the post of commandant of the guards at Xu, but he died before he could take up the office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]沛王琮相也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Chancellor to King Cong of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He was chancellor to Liu Cong, king of Pei.

sentences.zh: 琮，光武八代孫也。

translations.literal: Cong was eighth-generation descendant of Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Cong was an eighth-generation descendant of Emperor Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]縣名，城在今越州余姚縣西。

translations.literal: Comment [2] County name; the walled town is today west of Yuyao county in Yue prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Shangyu was a county whose old seat lay west of present Yuyao in Yue prefecture.

sentences.zh: 注[三]王朗字景興，肅之父也，《魏志》有傳。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Wang Lang's courtesy name was Jingxing, father of Su; Wei Records has his biography.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Wang Lang, style Jingxing, was Wang Su's father; his life appears in the Records of Wei.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰「忠乘船載笠蓋詣朗，見朗左右僮從皆著青絳采衣，非其奢麗，即辭疾發而退」也。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says "Zhong rode a boat carrying bamboo hat and canopy to visit Lang; seeing Lang's left and right pages and followers all wearing blue and crimson figured silks, not their luxury and splendor, immediately claimed illness and departed back."
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng relates that Yuan Zhong sailed to call on Wang Lang in hat and parasol, saw the governor's boys in gaudy silks, found the display intolerable, feigned illness, and left at once.

sentences.zh: 忠弟弘

translations.literal: Zhong's younger brother Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong (courtesy Shaofu), younger brother of Yuan Zhong.

sentences.zh: 弘字邵甫，恥其門族貴埶，乃變姓名，徒步師門，不應徵辟，終於家。

translations.literal: Hong's courtesy name was Shaofu; ashamed of his gate clan's noble power, he changed name and surname, went on foot to teacher's gate, did not answer summons and appointments, ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: This Yuan Hong, style Shaofu, shamed by his clan's eminence, lived under an alias, walked to study with a master, ignored every appointment, and died a private citizen.
Editorial note [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「弘嘗入京師太學，其從父逢為太尉，呼弘與相見。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie Cheng's book says: "Hong once entered the capital Taixue; his uncle by the father's line Feng was grand commandant; he called Hong to meet with him.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xie Cheng writes that Yuan Hong once entered the imperial academy while his cousin Yuan Feng was grand commandant; Feng summoned him to an audience.

sentences.zh: 遇逢宴會作樂，弘伏稱頭痛，不聽*

translations.literal: Encountering Feng at a banquet with music performing, Hong prostrated himself claiming headache, would not listen
translations.idiomatic: At one of Yuan Feng's musical banquets Yuan Hong collapsed feigning a violent headache and refused

translations.literal: (Text reads 呼 'summon'.)
translations.idiomatic: (Variant text inserts the verb "to summon.")

sentences.zh: **[音]*聲而退，遂不復往。

translations.literal: to obey the summons to stay and listen; he excused himself, withdrew, and never returned.
translations.idiomatic: He would not stay when summoned back to the banquet, pleaded his headache, and left for good.

sentences.zh: 紹、術兄弟亦不與通。」

translations.literal: The brothers Shao and Shu also did not communicate with him."
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao and Yuan Shu likewise cut him dead."

sentences.zh: 忠子秘

translations.literal: Zhong's son Mi.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Mi, son of Yuan Zhong.

sentences.zh: 忠子秘，為郡門下議生。

translations.literal: Zhong's son Mi was discussing student at the headquarters gate of the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Mi, Yuan Zhong's son, was a student adviser at the commandery gate.

sentences.zh: 黃巾起，秘從太守趙謙擊之，軍敗，秘與功曹封觀等七人以身扞刃，皆死於陳，謙以得免。

translations.literal: When Yellow Turbans arose, Mi followed grand administrator Zhao Qian to strike them; the army was defeated; Mi together with merit assessor Feng Guan and five other men used their bodies to ward the blades; all seven died in the line; Qian thereby obtained escape.
translations.idiomatic: When the rebellion broke out, Yuan Mi rode with Governor Zhao Qian against the rebels; the army broke; Yuan Mi, merit assessor Feng Guan, and five companions threw themselves on the enemy blades and died in formation so Zhao Qian could escape.

sentences.zh: 詔秘等門閭號曰「七賢」。

translations.literal: An edict on Mi and others' gate lanes titled them "Seven Worthies."
translations.idiomatic: The court honored their lanes with the title "the Seven Worthies."

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]謝承書曰「秘字永寧。

translations.literal: [1] Comment [1] Xie Cheng's book says "Mi's courtesy name was Yongning.
