translations.idiomatic: [1] Note [1]: Xie Cheng gives Yuan Mi's style as Yongning.

sentences.zh: 封觀與主簿陳端、門下督范仲禮、賊曹劉偉德、主記史丁子嗣、記室史張仲然、議生袁秘等七人擢刃突陳，與戰並死」也。

translations.literal: Feng Guan together with chief clerk Chen Duan, gate supervisor Fan Zhongli, bandit bureau Liu Weide, chief recorder Ding Zisi, secretary Zhang Zhongran, discussing student Yuan Mi and seven others drew blades and burst the line, fought together and all died."
translations.idiomatic: Feng Guan, chief clerk Chen Duan, gate captain Fan Zhongli, bandit-crew officer Liu Weide, chief recorder Ding Zisi, secretary Zhang Zhongran, and student Yuan Mi—seven men—drew swords, charged the enemy line, and perished together."

sentences.zh: 封觀者，有志節，當舉孝廉，以兄名位未顯，恥先受之，遂稱風疾，喑不能言。

translations.literal: Feng Guan was a man who had aspiration and integrity; when about to be recommended as filial and incorrupt, because his elder brother's name and position were not yet illustrious, ashamed to receive it first, thereupon claimed wind illness, mute and unable to speak.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Guan had high principles; when nominated as filial and incorrupt he was ashamed to accept before his elder brother was known, so he feigned a paralyzing wind illness and could not speak.

sentences.zh: 火起觀屋，徐出避之。

translations.literal: Fire rose on Guan's house; he slowly went out to avoid it.
translations.idiomatic: When fire broke out on his roof he strolled out unhurriedly.

sentences.zh: 忍而不告。

translations.literal: He endured and did not tell.
translations.idiomatic: He told no one, though he could have cried for help.

sentences.zh: 後數年，兄得舉，觀乃稱損而仕郡焉。

translations.literal: After several years the elder brother obtained recommendation; Guan then claimed recovery and took office in the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, once his brother had been nominated, he declared himself cured and entered commandery service.
Editorial note [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「觀字孝起，南頓人也。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie Cheng's book says: "Guan's courtesy name was Xiaoqi; he was a man of Nandun."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xie Cheng identifies Feng Guan, style Xiaoqi, as a man of Nandun.

sentences.zh: 論曰：陳平多陰謀，而知其後必廢；

translations.literal: Discourse says: Chen Ping had many yin plots, yet knew his posterity would be abolished;
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: Chen Ping trafficked in dark stratagems, yet foresaw his line would fall;

sentences.zh: [一]邴吉有陰德，夏侯勝識其當封及子孫。

translations.literal: [1] Bing Ji had yin virtue; Xiahou Sheng recognized he would be enfeoffed reaching sons and grandsons.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Bing Ji stored up hidden grace; Xiahou Sheng predicted ennoblement for his descendants.

sentences.zh: [二]終陳掌不侯，而邴昌紹國，雖有不類，未可致詰，其大致歸然矣。

translations.literal: [2] In the end Chen Zhang did not become marquis, yet Bing Chang continued the state; although the cases are not of the same kind, one cannot press for exact parallel; the great tendency returns thus.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Chen Zhang never won a marquisate while Bing Chang inherited a fief—the outcomes do not mirror each other in every point, yet the moral pattern is the same.

sentences.zh: 袁公竇氏之閒，乃情帝室，[三]引義雅正，可謂王臣之烈。

translations.literal: Between Lord Yuan and the Dou clan, then feeling for the imperial house; [3] drawing on meaning elegant and correct, can be called the fierceness of a king's minister.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Between Yuan An and the Dou clan his heart stayed with the throne; he argued from principle with a minister's steel.

sentences.zh: [四]及其理楚獄，未嘗鞫人於臧罪，其仁心足以覃乎後昆。

translations.literal: [4] As to his ordering the Chu prisons, he never interrogated a man on a corruption charge; his humane heart sufficed to extend to later descendants.
translations.idiomatic: [4] In the Chu trials he never tortured anyone on a graft charge; that humanity reached far down his line.

sentences.zh: [五]子孫之盛，不亦宜乎？

translations.literal: [5] Posterity's flourishing—was it not fitting?
translations.idiomatic: [5] Small wonder his descendants flourished.
Editorial note [6].

sentences.zh: 注[一]丞相陳平為高祖謀臣，出六奇，歎曰：「我多陰謀，道家之所禁，吾世即廢，以吾多陰謀禍也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Chancellor Chen Ping was Gaozu's strategist, produced six marvels, sighed: "I have many yin plots; what the Dao school forbids; my generation will then be abolished—because I have many yin plots it is misfortune."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Chancellor Chen Ping, architect of six stratagems for Gaozu, lamented that his love of secret schemes would doom his house, as Daoist teaching warns.

sentences.zh: 」其後曾孫掌以□氏親戚貴達，願得續封，而終不得也。

translations.literal: " Later great-grandson Zhang because of □ clan kinship was noble and reached, wished to obtain continued enfeoffment, yet in the end did not obtain it.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandson Chen Zhang later sought to renew the marquisate through imperial kinship but never received it.

sentences.zh: 注[二]武帝末，戾太子巫蠱事起，邴吉為廷尉監。

translations.literal: Comment [2] At the end of Wudi, when the heir apparent's witchcraft affair arose, Bing Ji was jail overseer of the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Near the end of Emperor Wu's reign, during the witchcraft case against the heir, Bing Ji served as jail overseer under the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 時宣帝年二歲，坐太子事系。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Xuan was two years old; because of the heir affair he was bound.
translations.idiomatic: The future Emperor Xuan was two years old and imprisoned as collateral damage.

sentences.zh: 望氣者言長安獄中有天子氣，於是上遣使者分條中都官詔獄，系者亡輕重一切皆殺之。

translations.literal: A qi-watcher said in Chang'an prisons there was Son of Heaven qi; therefore the emperor sent envoys to list out capital officials' edict prisons; those bound, without regard to light or heavy, all were to be killed.
translations.idiomatic: A court astrologer reported imperial aura in the Chang'an jails, so the throne ordered every prisoner in the capital prisons executed without distinction of guilt.

sentences.zh: 內者令郭穰至郡邸獄，吉閉門扞拒曰：「它人無辜猶不可，況親曾孫乎？」

translations.literal: Palace supply intendant Guo Rang reached the commandery hostelry prison; Ji closed the gate and resisted, saying: "Other men without guilt still may not; how much less a close great-grandson?"
translations.idiomatic: When Guo Rang reached the hostel jail, Bing Ji barred the gate: "You cannot kill the innocent—least of all the emperor's own great-grandson."

sentences.zh: 穰不得入，還以聞。

translations.literal: Rang could not enter; returned and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Rang could not get in and reported back.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「天使之也。

translations.literal: The emperor said: "Heaven caused it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Heaven meant to spare him.

sentences.zh: 」因大赦天下。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he issued a great amnesty for all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then proclaimed a general amnesty for the realm.

sentences.zh: 曾孫賴吉得立。

translations.literal: The great-grandson relied on Ji and was able to be established.
translations.idiomatic: The boy lived because of Bing Ji and eventually ascended the throne.

sentences.zh: 宣帝立，吉為丞相，未及封而病。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan was established, Ji was chancellor; before enfeoffment he fell ill.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan made Bing Ji chancellor; he fell ill before receiving a fief.

sentences.zh: 上憂吉不起，夏侯勝曰：「此未死也。

translations.literal: The emperor worried Ji would not rise; Xiahou Sheng said: "This one has not died.
translations.idiomatic: The court feared he would die; Xiahou Sheng said, "He is not finished yet.

sentences.zh: 臣聞有陰德者必饗其樂以及子孫。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that one who has yin virtue must enjoy his joy reaching sons and grandsons.
translations.idiomatic: Men who store hidden grace enjoy lasting blessing in their posterity.

sentences.zh: 」後吉病癒，封博陽侯。

translations.literal: " Later Ji's illness healed; he was enfeoffed marquis of Boyang.
translations.idiomatic: " Bing Ji recovered and was enfeoffed as marquis of Boyang.

sentences.zh: 薨，子顯嗣。

translations.literal: He died; son Xian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He died and was succeeded by his son Bing Xian.

sentences.zh: 甘露中，削爵為關內侯。

translations.literal: In the middle of Ganlu, title was cut to marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: During the Ganlu era his rank was reduced to marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 至孫昌，復封博陽侯。

translations.literal: Reaching grandson Chang, again was enfeoffed marquis of Boyang.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson Bing Chang later regained the Boyang marquisate.

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫，王莽敗乃絕。

translations.literal: It passed to sons reaching grandsons; when Wang Mang fell then it ended.
translations.idiomatic: The line ran through sons and grandsons until Wang Mang fell and the title lapsed.

sentences.zh: 注[三]乃情猶竭情也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] 乃情 is like 竭情 (exhaust feeling).
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The gloss equates the phrase with "to give one's whole heart."

sentences.zh: 注[四]易曰：「王臣蹇蹇，匪躬之故。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Changes says: "The king's minister is troubled, troubled—not for his own body's sake."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Book of Changes line, "The king's minister struggles—never for private gain," applies here.

sentences.zh: 」烈，業也。

translations.literal: " Fierce means enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss defines the closing word as achievement or devoted service.

sentences.zh: 注[五]爾雅曰：「覃，延也。」

translations.literal: Comment [5] Erya says: "覃 means extend."
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: The Erya glosses the word as "to extend."

sentences.zh: 注[六]此論並華嶠之詞也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] This discourse is all Hua Qiao's words.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: This summation follows Hua Qiao's wording.

sentences.zh: 張酺字孟侯，汝南細陽人，趙王張敖之後也。

translations.literal: Zhang Hao's courtesy name was Meng-hou; he was a man of Xiyang in Runan; descendant of Zhao king Zhang Ao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao, style Meng-hou, came from Xiyang in Runan and traced his descent to King Zhang Ao of Zhao.

sentences.zh: [一]敖子壽，封細陽之池陽鄉，後廢，因家焉。

translations.literal: [1] Ao's son Shou was enfeoffed at Chiyang village of Xiyang; later abolished; therefore family settled there.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Zhang Ao's son Zhang Shou held a fief at Chiyang in Xiyang; when it was revoked the family remained there.

sentences.zh: 注[一]敖父耳，自楚降漢，高祖封為趙王。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ao's father Er, from Chu submitting to Han, Gaozu enfeoffed as king of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Zhang Ao's father Zhang Er had defected from Chu to Han; Gaozu made him king of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 敖嗣，後有罪，廢為宣平侯。

translations.literal: Ao succeeded; later had crime, abolished as marquis of Xuanping.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ao inherited the throne, later lost it for a crime, and was demoted to marquis of Xuanping.

sentences.zh: 酺少從祖父充受尚書，能傳其業。

translations.literal: In youth Hao followed grandfather by the father's line Chong in receiving the Documents, able to transmit his occupation.
translations.idiomatic: As a boy Zhang Hao studied the Book of Documents under his grandfather Zhang Chong and mastered that tradition.

sentences.zh: [一]又事太常桓榮。

translations.literal: [1] Also he served minister of the imperial clan Huan Rong.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He also studied under Minister Huan Rong of the imperial clan.

sentences.zh: 勤力不怠，聚徒以百數。

translations.literal: Diligent in strength and not slack; he gathered disciples numbering in the hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: He worked tirelessly and drew hundreds of students.

sentences.zh: 永平九年，顯宗為四姓小侯開學於南宮，[二]置五經師。

translations.literal: In the ninth year of Yongping, Xianzong for the four-surname minor marquises opened study in the Southern Palace; [2] he set masters for the Five Classics.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 9 Emperor Ming opened a school in the Southern Palace for the four great consort clans' cadet marquises; [2] he appointed lecturers on each of the Five Classics.

sentences.zh: 酺以尚書教授，數講於御前。

translations.literal: Hao by the Documents taught; repeatedly lectured before the imperial presence.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao lectured on the Documents and was often called to expound before the throne.

sentences.zh: 以論難當意，除為郎，賜車馬衣裳，遂令入授皇太子。

translations.literal: Because in debate and refutation he hit the mark, he was appointed gentleman, was gifted carriage, horses, and robes, and was then ordered to enter and instruct the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: His disputations pleased the emperor, who named him a gentleman, gave him a carriage, horses, and robes, and assigned him to tutor the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《東觀記》曰：「充與光武同門學，光武即位，求問充，充已死。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Eastern Lodge Record says: "Chong studied in the same school as Guangwu; when Guangwu took the throne he sought Chong; Chong had already died."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Eastern Lodge Record states that Zhang Chong had been Guangwu's classmate; when Guangwu became emperor he looked for Chong, but Chong was already dead.

sentences.zh: 注[二]小侯，解見

translations.literal: Comment [2] Minor marquises, explanation see
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: On the "minor marquises," see

sentences.zh: 〈明紀〉

translations.literal: Annals of Ming
translations.idiomatic: the Annals of Emperor Ming

sentences.zh: 也。

translations.literal: also.
translations.idiomatic: for the full explanation.

sentences.zh: 酺為人質直，守經義，每侍講閒隙，數有匡正之辭，以嚴見憚。

translations.literal: Hao in person was plain and straight, guarded classic meaning; whenever attending lectures in gaps of time, repeatedly had words of correction and rectification; by severity he was feared.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao was blunt and principled; during breaks in the lectures he often corrected errors and his stern manner made others uneasy.

sentences.zh: [一]及肅宗即位，擢酺為侍中、虎賁中郎將。

translations.literal: [1] When Suzong took the throne, he raised Hao to attendant at the palace and chief commandant among the gentlemen of the household.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When Emperor Zhang succeeded, he promoted Zhang Hao to palace attendant and chief commandant of the household gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 數月，出為東郡太守。

translations.literal: Several months later he went out as grand administrator of Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later he was posted grand administrator of Dong.

sentences.zh: 酺自以嘗經親近，未悟見出，意不自得，[二]上疏辭曰：「臣愚以經術給事左右，少不更職，不曉文法，猥當剖符典郡，班政千里，必有負恩辱位之咎。

translations.literal: Hao himself thought he had once passed through close attendance, did not understand being sent out, intent was not self-content; [2] he submitted a memorial declining, saying: "Your servant stupidly by classics learning has served at left and right; in youth I did not rotate through offices, do not understand statutes and ordinances; rashly I bear split tallies to govern a commandery, to distribute government a thousand li—there must be the fault of betraying grace and shaming the post.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao had expected to stay at court; the sudden transfer unsettled him, [2] so he memorialized to decline: "I have served near the throne only as a classicist, with no broad administrative experience; to take the seal of a commandery and govern a thousand li invites failure and disgrace.

sentences.zh: 臣竊私自分，殊不慮出城闕，冀蒙留恩，托備□官，腢僚所不安，耳目所聞見，不敢避好醜。

translations.literal: Your servant privately divides himself, utterly does not consider leaving the capital walls; hopes to receive retained grace, to rely on serving in a reserve office—what colleagues would not be at ease with, what ears and eyes have heard and seen, I dare not avoid good or ugly."
translations.idiomatic: I never meant to leave the capital; I hoped to remain in a minor court post where I could speak plainly on whatever I saw or heard, fair or foul."

sentences.zh: 」詔報曰：「經云：『身雖在外，乃心不離王室。

translations.literal: " An edict replied: "The classic says: 'Though the body is outside, then the heart does not leave the royal house."
translations.idiomatic: "The rescript answered, "The canon says, Though your body be far away, your heart must not leave the royal house."

sentences.zh: 』[三]典城臨民，益所以報效也。

translations.literal: " [3] To hold a city and face the people is all the more the means to repay effect.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Governing the people from a commandery is itself the way to repay the throne.

sentences.zh: 好醜必上，不在遠近。

translations.literal: Good and ugly must ascend; it is not in far or near.
translations.idiomatic: Report every good or ill you find; distance does not matter.

sentences.zh: [四]今賜裝錢三十萬，其亟之官。

translations.literal: [4] Now we gift thirty thousand cash for travel gear; hasten to office."
translations.idiomatic: [4] Here are thirty thousand cash for your journey—go to your post at once."

sentences.zh: 」酺雖儒者，而性剛斷。

translations.literal: " Although Hao was a Ru, his nature was firm and decisive.
translations.idiomatic: Though a scholar, Zhang Hao was resolute by nature.

sentences.zh: 下車擢用義勇，搏擊豪強。

translations.literal: Descending from the carriage he raised and employed the righteous and brave, struck and attacked powerful magnates.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival he promoted stalwart men and moved against great bullies.

sentences.zh: 長安有殺盜徒者，酺輒案之，以為令長受臧，猶不至死，盜徒皆饑寒傭保，何足窮其法乎！

translations.literal: In Chang'an there was one who killed a band of robbers; Hao thereupon investigated it, considering that magistrates receiving corruption still did not reach death; robbers were all hungry and cold hired laborers—what sufficed to exhaust the law on them!
translations.idiomatic: When someone in Chang'an slaughtered a gang of thieves, Zhang Hao reviewed the case: a corrupt magistrate might not deserve death, but these were starving hirelings—why stretch the statute to the limit against them?

sentences.zh: 注[一]《東觀記曰：「太子家時為奢侈物，未嘗不正諫，甚見重焉。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Eastern Lodge Record says: "When the heir's household at times made luxury goods, he never failed to remonstrate straight; he was greatly valued."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Eastern Lodge Record says that whenever the crown prince's household ordered luxuries, Zhang Hao remonstrated without fail and was deeply respected.

sentences.zh: 注[二]悟，曉也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wu means understand.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The gloss defines the word as to understand or perceive.

sentences.zh: 注[三]《尚書》康王之誥曰「雖爾身在外，乃心罔不在王室」也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] The Documents, Announcement of King Kang says "Though your bodies are outside, your hearts are nowhere not in the royal house."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The Book of Documents, in King Kang's announcement, reads, "Though you serve abroad, your hearts never leave the royal house."

sentences.zh: 注[四]好醜謂善惡也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Good and ugly means good and evil.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: "Good and ugly" here means good and bad conduct.

sentences.zh: 言事之善惡，必以聞上，此即報效，豈拘外內也。

translations.literal: To speak of affairs' good and evil must thereby be heard above; this then is repaying effect—how can it be constrained to outer or inner?
translations.idiomatic: Whatever happens, good or evil, must reach the throne; that is loyal service, whether at court or in the provinces.

sentences.zh: 郡吏王青者，[一]祖父翁，與前太守翟義起兵攻王莽，及義敗，余觿悉降，翁獨守節力戰，莽遂燔燒之。

translations.literal: Commandery clerk Wang Qing; [1] grandfather Weng, with former grand administrator Zhai Yi raised troops to attack Wang Mang; when Yi was defeated, the remaining host all surrendered; Weng alone guarded integrity and fought with strength; Mang thereupon burned him alive.
translations.idiomatic: Among his clerks was Wang Qing; [1] his grandfather Wang Weng had joined Governor Zhai Yi's rebellion against Wang Mang; when Yi fell the survivors surrendered, but Weng fought to the end and Mang had him burned alive.

sentences.zh: 父隆，建武初為都尉功曹，青為小史。

translations.literal: Father Long, in early Jianwu was merit assessor for the commandant; Qing was petty clerk.
translations.idiomatic: His father Wang Long had been merit assessor to the commandant in early Jianwu; Wang Qing himself was a junior clerk.

sentences.zh: 與父俱從都尉行縣，道遇賊，隆以身□全都尉，遂死於難；

translations.literal: Together with father both followed the commandant on circuit of counties; on the road they met bandits; Long with body □ protected the commandant entirely, thereupon died in the disaster;
translations.idiomatic: Father and son accompanied the commandant on an inspection tour; bandits ambushed them; Wang Long shielded the commandant with his body and died;

sentences.zh: 青亦被矢貫咽，音聲流喝。

translations.literal: Qing also was pierced by an arrow through the throat; voice flowed hoarse.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Qing took an arrow through the throat and his voice was reduced to a rasp.

sentences.zh: [二] 前郡守以青身有金夷，竟不能舉。

translations.literal: [2] Former commandery keeper because Qing's body had metal wounds, in the end could not recommend him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The previous governor had never nominated him because of his battle scars.

sentences.zh: [三]酺見之，歎息曰：「豈有一門忠義而爵賞不及乎？

translations.literal: [3] Hao saw him, sighed, saying: "How can one gate be loyal and righteous yet rank and reward not reach them?"
translations.idiomatic: [3] When Zhang Hao met him he exclaimed, "Can a house of such loyalty go unrewarded?"

sentences.zh: 」遂擢用極右曹，[四]乃上疏薦青三世死節，宜蒙顯異。

translations.literal: " Thereupon promoted and employed him to the highest staff rank on the right; [4] then submitted memorial recommending Qing three generations died for integrity, fitting to receive manifest distinction.
translations.idiomatic: " He gave Wang Qing a senior staff appointment, [4] then memorialized that three generations of his family had died for loyalty and deserved extraordinary honors.

sentences.zh: 奏下三公，由此為司空所辟。

translations.literal: Memorial sent down to the Three Dukes; thereby he was summoned by the minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial went to the Three Dukes, and Wang Qing was recruited by the minister of works.
Editorial note [5].

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「青字公然，東郡聊城人也。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie Cheng's book says: "Qing's courtesy name was Gongran; he was a man of Liaocheng in Dong commandery."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Xie Cheng gives Wang Qing, style Gongran, as a native of Liaocheng in Dong.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「流」或作「嘶」。

translations.literal: Comment [2] "Flow" sometimes is written "嘶" (hoarse).
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Some texts read "hoarse" instead of "flowing" for his voice.

sentences.zh: 喝音一介反。

translations.literal: 喝 is read with fanqie 一 介.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the fanqie reading for the character describing his voice.

sentences.zh: 廣蒼曰：「聲之幽也。」

translations.literal: Guangcang says: "The darkness of sound."
translations.idiomatic: The Guangcang defines the term as a hollow, dying sound.

sentences.zh: 注[三]夷，傷也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Yi means wound.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Here the gloss means wound or scar.

sentences.zh: 注[四]漢官儀曰：「督郵﹑功曹，郡之極位。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] Han Official Rites says: "Postal inspectors and merit assessors are the commandery's supreme posts."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Han Official Rites lists postal inspector and merit assessor as the commandery's highest staff offices.

sentences.zh: 注[五]《東觀記》曰「青從此除步兵司馬。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Eastern Lodge Record says "Qing from this was appointed infantry commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: The Eastern Lodge Record says Wang Qing was then named infantry commandant

sentences.zh: 酺傷青不遂，復舉其子孝廉」也。

translations.literal: Hao grieved that Qing did not advance, again recommended his son as filial and incorrupt."
translations.idiomatic: and that Zhang Hao, sorry Wang Qing had not risen higher, also nominated his son as filial and incorrupt."

sentences.zh: 自酺出後，帝每見諸王師傅，常言：「張酺前入侍講，屢有諫正，誾誾惻惻，出於誠心，可謂有史魚之風矣。

translations.literal: From after Hao went out, the emperor whenever he saw tutors to the kings always said: "Zhang Hao formerly entered to attend lectures; repeatedly had remonstrance and correction; earnest and earnest, compassionate and compassionate, coming from sincere heart—can be called to have the wind of Shi Yu."
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Hao left the capital, the emperor often told the princes' tutors, "Zhang Hao used to lecture the heir with blunt, heartfelt remonstrance—true Shi Yu spirit."

sentences.zh: 」[一]元和二年，東巡狩，幸東郡，引酺及門生並郡縣掾史並會庭中。

translations.literal: " [1] In the second year of Yuanhe, eastward hunting inspection, he favored Dong commandery, led Hao and disciples together with commandery and county clerks all to meet in the courtyard.
translations.idiomatic: [1] In Yuanhe 2, on an eastern tour he visited Dong commandery, summoned Zhang Hao, his students, and the local staff to an audience in the courtyard.

sentences.zh: 帝先備弟子之儀，使酺講尚書一篇，然後修君臣之禮。

translations.literal: The emperor first prepared the disciple's rites, had Hao lecture one chapter of the Documents, then practiced the rites of lord and minister.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor first received him as a disciple while Zhang Hao expounded one chapter of the Documents, then they observed the etiquette of sovereign and subject.

sentences.zh: [二] 賞賜殊特，莫不沾洽。

translations.literal: [2] Rewards and gifts were exceptionally special; none did not receive moistening.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The gifts were lavish and everyone present went away enriched.

sentences.zh: 注[一]誾誾，忠正也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] 誾誾 means loyal and upright.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The reduplication describes loyal, forthright speech.

sentences.zh: 惻惻，懇切也。

translations.literal: 惻惻 means earnest and urgent.
translations.idiomatic: The second pair describes deep, urgent sincerity.

sentences.zh: 史魚，衛大夫，名□，字子魚。

translations.literal: Shi Yu was a grandee of Wei, name □, courtesy name Ziyu.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Yu was a minister of Wei, known as Ziyu.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰「直哉史魚，邦有道如矢，邦無道如矢」也。

translations.literal: Confucius said "Straight indeed was Shi Yu; when the state had the Way he was like an arrow; when the state lacked the Way he was like an arrow."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said of him, "How straight was Shi Yu! In an ordered age he was straight as an arrow; in a disordered age he was straight as an arrow."

sentences.zh: 注[二]《東觀記》曰：「時使尚書令王鮪與酺相難，上甚欣悅。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Eastern Lodge Record says: "At the time they ordered Director of the Masters Wang Wan to dispute with Hao; the emperor was greatly pleased."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Eastern Lodge Record adds that Director Wang Wan was ordered to debate Zhang Hao, to the emperor's delight.

sentences.zh: 酺視事十五年，和帝初，遷魏郡太守。

translations.literal: Hao held office fifteen years; at He Emperor's beginning, transferred to grand administrator of Wei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: After fifteen years in Dong he was moved to grand administrator of Wei under Emperor He.

sentences.zh: 郡人鄭據時為司隸校尉，奏免執金吾竇景。

translations.literal: Commandery man Zheng Ju at the time was metropolitan commandant; he memorialized to dismiss bearer of the golden mace Dou Jing.
translations.idiomatic: His townsman Zheng Ju was metropolitan commandant and had impeached Dou Jing, bearer of the golden mace, out of office.

sentences.zh: 景後復位，遣掾夏猛私謝酺曰：「鄭據小人，為所侵冤。

translations.literal: Jing later returned to position, sent clerk Xia Meng privately to thank Hao, saying: "Zheng Ju is a petty man; he was wronged by what he suffered.
translations.idiomatic: When Dou Jing was restored, he sent clerk Xia Meng to Zhang Hao with thanks and a message: "Zheng Ju is a nobody who slandered me unfairly.

sentences.zh: 聞其兒為吏，放縱狼藉。

translations.literal: Hearing his son is an official, unrestrained and disorderly.
translations.idiomatic: I hear his son holds office and behaves outrageously.

sentences.zh: 取是曹子一人，足以驚百。

translations.literal: Take one of that fellow's sons—suffices to frighten a hundred."
translations.idiomatic: Arrest one of that man's sons and you will terrify a hundred evildoers."

sentences.zh: 」酺大怒，即收猛系獄，檄言執金吾府，疑猛與據子不平，矯稱卿意，以報私讎。

translations.literal: " Hao greatly angry, immediately seized Meng and bound in prison, circulated document to bearer of the golden mace's office, suspecting Meng and Ju's son were not at peace, falsely claimed your lordship's intent, to repay private hatred.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao was furious: he jailed Xia Meng and notified Dou Jing's office that the clerk had probably forged Dou's orders out of private spite against Zheng Ju's son.

sentences.zh: 會有贖罪令，猛乃得出。

translations.literal: It happened there was an edict on redeeming crimes; Meng then was able to come out.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty for commutable offenses soon freed Xia Meng.

sentences.zh: [一]頃之，征入為河南尹。

translations.literal: [1] Shortly he was summoned in as governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Shortly afterward he was recalled to be governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 竇景家人復擊傷市卒，吏捕得之，景怒，遣緹騎侯海等五百人歐傷市丞。

translations.literal: Dou Jing's household people again struck and wounded market constables; clerks arrested and got them; Jing angry, sent five hundred cavalry in crimson silk Hou Hai and others beat and wounded the market assistant.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Jing's retainers beat market guards; when the guards arrested them, Dou Jing sent five hundred crimson-clad horsemen under Hou Hai to thrash the market superintendent.

sentences.zh: [二]酺部吏楊章等窮究，正海罪，徙朔方。

translations.literal: [2] Hao's department clerks Yang Zhang and others exhaustively investigated, fixed Hai's crime, banished to Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Zhang Hao's subordinates Yang Zhang and others ran the case to ground, convicted Hou Hai, and sent him to Shuofang.

sentences.zh: 景忿怨，乃移書辟章等六人為執金吾吏，欲因報之。

translations.literal: Jing resentful, thereupon sent a letter summoning Zhang and six others as clerks to bearer of the golden mace, wishing thereby to retaliate.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Jing, enraged, tried to summon Zhang and six others into his own service so he could ruin them.

sentences.zh: 章等惶恐，入白酺，願自引臧罪，以辭景命。

translations.literal: Zhang and others fearful, entered and reported to Hao, wished themselves to admit corruption crime, to decline Jing's order.
translations.idiomatic: They fled to Zhang Hao in terror and offered to confess bribery rather than obey Dou Jing's summons.

sentences.zh: 酺即上言其狀。

translations.literal: Hao immediately ascended speech on the situation.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao at once memorialized the facts.

sentences.zh: 竇太后詔報：「自今執金吾辟吏，皆勿遣。」

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Dou's edict replied: "From now when bearer of the golden mace summons clerks, all do not dispatch."
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Dou replied by edict: "Henceforth no clerk may be transferred to the bearer of the golden mace's staff on such orders."

sentences.zh: 注[一]《東觀記》曰「據字平卿，黎陽人也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Eastern Lodge Record says "Ju's courtesy name was Pingqing; he was a man of Liyang."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Eastern Lodge Record gives Zheng Ju, style Pingqing, as a man of Liyang.

sentences.zh: 為侍御史，轉司隸校尉」也。

translations.literal: He was attendant censor, transferred to metropolitan commandant."
translations.idiomatic: He had served as attendant censor and metropolitan commandant."

sentences.zh: 注[二]《說文》曰：「緹，帛丹黃色也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Shuowen says: "Ti is silk of cinnabar-yellow color."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Shuowen defines ti silk as red-yellow.

sentences.zh: 」漢官儀曰，執金吾有緹騎。

translations.literal: " Han Official Rites says the bearer of the golden mace has 緹騎.
translations.idiomatic: The Han Official Rites notes that the bearer of the golden mace commands crimson-clad cavalry.

sentences.zh: 及竇氏敗，酺乃上疏曰：「臣實愚惷，不及大體，[一]以為竇氏雖伏厥辜，而罪刑未著，後世不見其事，但聞其誅，非所以垂示國典，貽之將來。

translations.literal: When the Dou clan fell, Hao then submitted memorial saying: "Your servant is truly stupid and dull, does not reach great pattern; [1] I thought that though the Dou clan has submitted to its guilt, yet crime and punishment are not yet manifest; later ages will not see the affair but only hear of their execution—not the means to hang forth the state's model and bequeath to the future.
translations.idiomatic: After the Dou clan's fall Zhang Hao wrote: "I may be slow to see the whole picture, [1] but even though the Dous have paid for their crimes, the record of their offenses is unclear; posterity will hear only that they were killed, not why—which does not serve the law or instruct the future.

sentences.zh: 宜下理官，與天下平之。

translations.literal: It is fitting to send down to the judging officials, with all under heaven level their case."
translations.idiomatic: Let the judiciary publish a balanced verdict for the realm."

sentences.zh: [二]方憲等寵貴，腢臣阿附唯恐不及，皆言憲受顧命之托，懷伊﹑呂之忠，[三]至乃復比鄧夫人於文母。

translations.literal: [2] When Dou Xian and others were favored and noble, all ministers curried favor fearing not to reach them, all saying Xian received the late emperor's testament trust, cherished Yi and Lü's loyalty; [3] they went so far again to compare Lady Deng to the cultured mother.
translations.idiomatic: [2] While Dou Xian was all-powerful, every official flattered him, claiming he held the late emperor's deathbed charge and rivaled Yi Yin and the Duke of Zhou; [3] some even likened Lady Deng to King Wen's consort.

sentences.zh: [四]今嚴威既行，皆言當死，不復顧其前後，考折厥衷。

translations.literal: [4] Now stern might already having been applied, all say they ought to die, no longer regard their before and after, examine and break their inner core.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Now that terror reigns, everyone cries death with no thought of weighing earlier service against later crime.

sentences.zh: 臣伏見夏陽侯纓，每存忠善，前與臣言，常有盡節之心，檢□賓客，未嘗犯法。

translations.literal: Your servant humbly sees Marquis of Xiayang Ying of the Dou clan, who always stores loyal goodness; formerly with your servant spoke, constantly had the heart to exhaust loyalty; in inspecting his retainers never violated law.
translations.idiomatic: I speak for Dou Ying, marquis of Xiayang: he has always meant well; he once told me he wished to serve loyally to the end, and I have watched his clients break no law.

sentences.zh: 臣聞王政骨肉之刑，有三宥之義，過厚不過薄。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that kingly government for flesh-and-blood punishments has the meaning of three pardons; excess thick does not pass thin.
translations.idiomatic: Royal justice toward kin allows three grounds for mercy; generosity should not slide into cruelty.

sentences.zh: [五]今議者為纓選嚴能相，恐其迫切，必不完免，宜裁加貸宥，以崇厚德。

translations.literal: [5] Now debaters for Ying select stern able chancellors, fearing their pressing urgency will certainly not completely spare him; it is fitting to trim and add indulgent pardon, to exalt thick virtue."
translations.idiomatic: [5] Those who would assign Dou Ying a harsh governor mean to hound him to death; I ask that he be shown leniency instead, in keeping with humane government."

sentences.zh: 」和帝感酺言，徙纓封，就國而己。

translations.literal: " He Emperor was moved by Hao's words; transferred Ying's fief; he went to state only.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He accepted Zhang Hao's plea: Dou Ying's fief was shifted and he was sent to his domain with his life spared.

sentences.zh: 注[一]鄭玄注《周禮》云：「惷愚，癡騃也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Zheng Xuan's commentary on Zhou Rites says: "惷愚 means dull and stupid."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Zheng Xuan glosses the phrase as "dull-witted."

sentences.zh: 」惷音陟降反。

translations.literal: " Chun is read with fanqie zhu jiang.
translations.idiomatic: The line gives the fanqie reading for the dull-witted character in the preceding gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[二]平之謂平論其罪也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Level them means level-discuss their crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: "Level" means to give a fair, public accounting of their crimes.

sentences.zh: 注[三]臨終之命曰顧命。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Charge at life's end is called testament charge.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: A ruler's final charge to his ministers is called the "testament charge."

sentences.zh: 注[四]臣賢案：鄧夫人即穰侯鄧疊母元也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Your servant Xian's case: Lady Deng is Marquis of Rang Deng Die's mother Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: Li Xian identifies "Lady Deng" as Yuan, mother of Deng Die, marquis of Rang.

sentences.zh: 元出入宮掖，共竇憲女豻郭舉父子同謀殺害，與竇氏同誅，語見

translations.literal: Yuan went in and out of the palace apartments, together with Dou Xian's daughter-in-law Guo Ju and father and son conspired murder; together with Dou clan was executed; the language is seen in
translations.idiomatic: She moved freely in the inner palace and with Dou Xian's daughter-in-law Guo Ju and her kin plotted murder; she perished with the Dous, as related in

sentences.zh: 〈憲傳〉

translations.literal: Biography of Xian
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian's biography

sentences.zh: ，故張酺論憲兼及其黨。

translations.literal: , therefore Zhang Hao in discussing Xian also reached his faction.
translations.idiomatic: —hence Zhang Hao's memorial on Dou Xian touched her clique as well.

sentences.zh: 稱鄧夫人者，猶如《前書》霍光妻稱霍顯，祁太伯母號祁夫人之類也。

translations.literal: Calling Lady Deng is like in Former Documents Huo Guang's wife called Huo Xian, Qi Taibo's mother titled Lady Qi and such categories.
translations.idiomatic: The title "Lady Deng" follows the Former Han usage for Huo Guang's wife Huo Xian or Lady Qi, not a formal queenly title.

sentences.zh: 文母，文王之妻也。

translations.literal: The cultured mother was King Wen's wife.
translations.idiomatic: The "cultured mother" means King Wen's consort.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「既有烈考，亦有文母。」

translations.literal: The Odes says: "We have a martial father; we also have a cultured mother."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, "We had a mighty father; we have a cultured mother as well."

sentences.zh: 注[五]《禮記》曰「公族有罪，獄成，有司讞於公曰：『某之罪在大辟。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Record of Rites says "When a ducal clansman had crime, when the prison was complete, the responsible official reported to the duke saying 'So-and-so's crime is in the great execution."
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: The Record of Rites describes how a ducal kinsman's capital case was reported: "The prisoner's offense carries the death penalty."

sentences.zh: 』公曰：『宥之。

translations.literal: " The duke said: "Pardon him."
translations.idiomatic: The duke answered, "Pardon him."

sentences.zh: 』有司又曰：『在大辟。

translations.literal: " The responsible official again said: "In the great execution."
translations.idiomatic: The officer repeated, "The crime is capital."

sentences.zh: 』公又曰：『宥之。

translations.literal: " The duke again said: "Pardon him."
translations.idiomatic: Again the duke said, "Pardon him."

sentences.zh: 』有司又曰：『在大辟。

translations.literal: " The responsible official again said: "In the great execution."
translations.idiomatic: A third time: "It is a capital crime."

sentences.zh: 』公又曰：『宥之。

translations.literal: " The duke again said: "Pardon him."
translations.idiomatic: A third pardon from the duke.

sentences.zh: 』及三宥不對，走出，致刑於甸人。

translations.literal: " When three pardons he did not answer, ran out, applied punishment to the field man.
translations.idiomatic: After the third pardon the officer left in silence and delegated execution to the steward of the ducal fields.

sentences.zh: 公又使人追之，曰：『雖然，必宥之。

translations.literal: The duke again sent a man to pursue him, saying: "Even so, must pardon him."
translations.idiomatic: The duke sent a runner: "Even so, you must spare him."

sentences.zh: 』有司曰：『無及也。

translations.literal: " The responsible official said: "There is no time."
translations.idiomatic: The officer replied, "It is too late."

sentences.zh: 』反命於公，公素服如其倫之喪」也。

translations.literal: " He returned report to the duke; the duke in plain white as for mourning of his peer class."
translations.idiomatic: He reported back; the duke donned white as for the death of a peer."

sentences.zh: 永元五年，遷酺為太僕。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongyuan, Hao was transferred to grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 5 Zhang Hao was promoted to grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 數月，代尹睦為太尉。

translations.literal: Several months later he replaced Yin Mu as grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later he succeeded Yin Mu as grand commandant.

sentences.zh: [一]數上疏以疾乞身，薦魏郡太守徐防自代。

translations.literal: [1] Repeatedly he submitted memorials asking to retire on grounds of illness and recommended Xu Fang, grand administrator of Wei, to replace him.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He repeatedly asked leave for illness and nominated Xu Fang of Wei to take his place.

sentences.zh: 帝不許，使中黃門問病，加以珍羞，賜錢三十萬。

translations.literal: The emperor did not permit it; he sent a middle yellow gate to inquire after his illness, added rare delicacies, and granted three hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused, sent a eunuch to ask after his health, sent costly food, and gave him three hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 酺遂稱篤。

translations.literal: Hao then claimed his illness was grave.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao then insisted he was desperately ill.

sentences.zh: 時子蕃以郎侍講，帝因令小黃門□蕃曰：「陰陽不和，萬人失所，朝廷望公思惟得失，與國同心，而托病自絜，求去重任，誰當與吾同憂責者？

translations.literal: At that time his son Fan as gentleman was attending the lectures; the emperor therefore ordered a junior yellow gate to tell Fan: "Yin and yang are not in harmony; the myriad people have lost their place; the court looks to your father to weigh gain and loss and share one heart with the state, yet he pleads illness to keep himself pure and seeks to leave a heavy post—who then will share my cares and responsibilities with me?
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhang Fan was lecturing at court as a gentleman; the emperor sent a junior eunuch to tell him, "Heaven and earth are out of joint and the people suffer; we need your father to weigh policy and stand with the throne, yet he hides behind sickness to shed a great burden—who then will bear this worry with me?

sentences.zh: 非有望於斷金也。

translations.literal: It is not that I have expectation of severing metal."
translations.idiomatic: I am not asking him for some oath of unbreakable friendship."

sentences.zh: [二]司徒固疾，司空年老，[三]公其傴僂，勿露所□。

translations.literal: " [2] The minister of education is gravely ill, the minister of works is old; [3] you, sir, should bow with deference and not disclose what you □."
translations.idiomatic: [2] The minister of education is bedridden and the minister of works is aged; [3] bend your back to duty and keep counsel to yourself."

sentences.zh: 」[四]酺惶恐詣闕謝，還復視事。

translations.literal: " [4] Hao in fear went to the palace gate to apologize, then returned and resumed his duties.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Zhang Hao, alarmed, apologized at the palace gate and went back to office.

sentences.zh: 酺雖在公位，而父常居田裡，酺每有遷職，輒一詣京師。

translations.literal: Although Hao held a public post, his father always lived in the countryside; whenever Hao received a promotion he would go once to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Though Zhang Hao served at court, his father stayed on the farm; every time Zhang Hao was promoted he made one trip to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 嘗來候酺，適會歲節，公卿罷朝，俱詣酺府奉酒上壽，極歡卒日，觿人皆慶羨之。

translations.literal: When the father once came to visit Hao, it happened to be the seasonal festival; the dukes had left court and all went to Hao's residence bearing wine and toasting his longevity; they were utterly merry all day; all men envied them.
translations.idiomatic: Once when his father visited him at the New Year, the high ministers had left court and all converged on Zhang Hao's house with toasts and good cheer; the whole company feasted the day away to universal envy.

sentences.zh: 及父卒，既葬，詔遣使繼牛酒為釋服。

translations.literal: When his father died, after burial an edict ordered envoys to follow with oxen and wine for the ceremony of laying aside mourning garments.
translations.idiomatic: After his father was buried, the court sent envoys with oxen and wine for the end-of-mourning rites.

sentences.zh: 注[一]漢官儀曰：「睦字伯師，河南鞏人也。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Han Official Rites says: "Mu's courtesy name was Boshi; he was a man of Gong in Henan."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Han Official Rites gives Yin Mu, style Boshi, as a native of Gong in Henan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]斷金，解在

translations.literal: Comment [2] "Severing metal," explanation see
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: On the phrase "severing metal," see

sentences.zh: 〈皇后紀〉

translations.literal: Annals of the Empresses
translations.idiomatic: the Annals of the Empresses

translations.literal: Period.
translations.idiomatic: End of the cross-reference.

sentences.zh: 注[三]時司徒劉方，司空張奮也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] At the time the minister of education was Liu Fang, the minister of works Zhang Fen.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The minister of education was Liu Fang and the minister of works Zhang Fen.

sentences.zh: 注[四]傴僂言恭敬從命也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] 傴僂 means to bow with respect and obey orders.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The phrase means to bow humbly and obey.

sentences.zh: 《左氏傳》曰：「一命而僂，再命而傴，三命而俯。」

translations.literal: Zuo Tradition says: "One command and he bowed; two commands and he bent double; three commands and he stooped low."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition says, "One appointment made him bow; two made him bend; three made him stoop."

sentences.zh: 後以事與司隸校尉晏稱會於朝堂，酺從容謂稱曰：「三府辟吏，多非其人。

translations.literal: Later on an occasion he met metropolitan commandant Yan Cheng in the court hall; Hao calmly said to Cheng: "The Three Offices' recruited clerks—many are not the right men for their posts.
translations.idiomatic: Later, meeting metropolitan commandant Yan Cheng in the hall of state, Zhang Hao remarked in an offhand way that too many of the Three Dukes' appointees were unfit.

sentences.zh: 」稱歸，即奏令三府各實其掾史。

translations.literal: " Cheng returned and immediately memorialized ordering the Three Offices each to verify their clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Cheng went straight home and memorialized that each of the Three Dukes must audit his staff.

sentences.zh: 酺本以私言，不意稱奏之，甚懷恨。

translations.literal: Hao had originally spoken as private talk; he did not expect Cheng to memorialize it; he greatly harbored resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Hao had spoken in confidence and never dreamed Yan Cheng would memorialize it; he nursed a bitter grudge.

sentences.zh: 會復共謝闕下，酺因責讓於稱。

translations.literal: When they happened again together to apologize below the palace gate, Hao therefore rebuked Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: When they next met at the palace gate to apologize together, Zhang Hao scolded Yan Cheng.

sentences.zh: 稱辭言不順，酺怒，遂廷叱之，稱乃劾奏酺有怨言。

translations.literal: Cheng's reply was not compliant; Hao grew angry and thereupon rebuked him in the courtyard; Cheng then impeached Hao for having resentful words.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Cheng answered insolently; Zhang Hao lost his temper and dressed him down in open court, so Yan Cheng impeached him for seditious language.

sentences.zh: 天子以酺先帝師，有詔公卿﹑博士﹑朝臣會議。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because Hao was the late emperor's teacher issued an edict for the dukes, erudits, and court officials to assemble in conference.
translations.idiomatic: Because Zhang Hao had tutored the late emperor, the throne ordered the ministers, academicians, and court to debate the charge.

sentences.zh: 司徒呂蓋奏酺位居三司，知公門有儀，不屏氣鞠躬以須詔命，反作色大言，怨讓使臣，不可以示四遠。

translations.literal: Minister of education Lü Gai memorialized that Hao held rank among the Three Offices, knew there was protocol at the public gate, yet did not hold his breath and bow to await edict command; instead he changed color and spoke loudly, resenting and blaming the imperial commissioner—this cannot be shown to the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: Minister of education Lü Gai argued that as a three-duke Zhang Hao knew court etiquette yet had not waited humbly for orders but had flushed and shouted at an imperial agent—conduct unworthy of a minister.

sentences.zh: [一]於是策免。

translations.literal: [1] Thereupon he was dismissed by edict.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The court dismissed him by edict.

sentences.zh: 注[一]司隸校尉督大奸猾，無所不察，故曰使臣也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The metropolitan commandant supervises great villains and slippery men; there is nothing he does not investigate; therefore he is called imperial commissioner.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The metropolitan commandant oversaw great malefactors and was known as the emperor's investigator.

sentences.zh: 乘輿縞素臨吊，賜頤塋地，賵贈恩寵異於它相。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage in white silk came in person to condole; he was granted burial ground for his tomb mound; funeral gifts and favor were exceptional compared to other chancellors.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor came in mourning white to mourn him, granted a tomb plot, and sent funeral gifts beyond the usual for a minister.

sentences.zh: 酺病臨危，□其子曰：「顯節陵埽地露祭，欲率天下以儉。

translations.literal: When Hao's illness was critical, □ his sons, saying: "At the Manifest Virtue mausoleum they sweep the ground and offer open-air sacrifice, wishing to lead all under heaven by frugality.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed Zhang Hao told his sons, "Emperor Ming's tomb is honored with a broomed-earth sacrifice in the open, to teach the empire thrift.

sentences.zh: [一]吾為三公，既不能宣揚王化，令吏人從制，豈可不務節約乎？

translations.literal: [1] I have been one of the Three Offices; if I cannot spread abroad kingly transformation and make clerks and people follow the regulations, how can I not practice economy?
translations.idiomatic: [1] I held one of the three highest posts; if I could not model the law for officials and commoners, how could I ignore frugality in death?

sentences.zh: 其無起祠堂，可作□蓋廡，施祭其下而已。

translations.literal: Do not raise a shrine hall; you may build a □ roofed corridor and offer sacrifice beneath it—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Do not build me a mortuary shrine—only a simple roofed gallery for offerings underneath."
[2] End of his testament.

sentences.zh: 注[一]顯節，明帝陵也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Manifest Virtue is Emperor Ming's mausoleum.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Manifest Virtue mausoleum is Emperor Ming's tomb.

sentences.zh: 明帝遺詔無起寢廟，故言埽地而祭也，故酺遵奉之。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming's testamentary edict ordered not to raise inner temple halls; therefore it speaks of sweeping the ground to sacrifice; therefore Hao followed and honored it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming had forbidden inner temples at his tomb, hence the earth-sweeping rite; Zhang Hao followed that example.

sentences.zh: 注[二]廡，屋也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wu means a roofed structure.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The word means a covered gallery, not a full hall.

sentences.zh: 曾孫濟

translations.literal: Great-grandson Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ji, great-grandson of Zhang Hao.

sentences.zh: 曾孫濟，好儒學，[一]光和中至司空，病罷。

translations.literal: Great-grandson Ji loved Ru learning; [1] in the Guanghe era he reached minister of works, then resigned on illness.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ji loved Confucian scholarship; [1] under Emperor Ling he rose to minister of works, then retired ill.

sentences.zh: 及卒，靈帝以舊恩贈車騎將軍﹑關內侯印綬。

translations.literal: When he died, Lingdi for old favor granted posthumous seals and ribbons of chariot-and-cavalry general and marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: When he died Emperor Ling, remembering old ties, posthumously awarded the seals of chariot-and-cavalry general and marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 其年，追濟侍講有勞，封子根為蔡陽鄉侯。

translations.literal: That year, tracing Ji's labor in attending lectures, he enfeoffed son Gen as village marquis of Caiyang.
translations.idiomatic: The same year the court enfeoffed his son Zhang Gen as village marquis of Caiyang for Zhang Ji's service as lecturer.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「蕃生盤，盤生濟。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Hua Qiao's book says: "Fan begat Pan, Pan begat Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Hua Qiao gives the line as Zhang Fan, then Zhang Pan, then Zhang Ji.

sentences.zh: 濟字符江。

translations.literal: Ji's courtesy name was Yuanjiang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ji bore the courtesy name Yuanjiang.

sentences.zh: 靈帝初，楊賜薦濟明習典訓，為侍講。」

translations.literal: At the beginning of Lingdi, Yang Ci recommended Ji as clear in classical instruction, and he became attendant lecturer."
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Ling's reign Yang Ci recommended him for his mastery of the canon, and he became court lecturer."

sentences.zh: 濟弟喜，初平中為司空。

translations.literal: Ji's younger brother Xi, in the middle of Chuping became minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Zhang Xi became minister of works during the Chuping years.

sentences.zh: 韓稜字伯師，穎川舞陽人，弓高侯頹當之後也。

translations.literal: Han Leng's courtesy name was Boshi; he was a man of Wuyang in Yingchuan; descendant of Marquis of Gonggao Tuidang.
translations.idiomatic: Han Leng, style Boshi, came from Wuyang in Yingchuan and traced his line to Marquis Tuidang of Gonggao.

sentences.zh: [一]世為鄉里著姓。

translations.literal: [1] For generations they were a noted surname in the village.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The family had been a leading clan in the district for generations.

sentences.zh: 父尋，建武中為隴西太守。

translations.literal: His father Xun, in Jianwu was grand administrator of Longxi.
translations.idiomatic: His father Han Xun had been grand administrator of Longxi under Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 注[一]頹當，韓王信之子。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Tuidang was son of King Xin of Han, Han Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Tuidang was a son of Han Xin, king of Han.

sentences.zh: 見《前書》。

translations.literal: See the Former Documents.
translations.idiomatic: See the Former Han history.

sentences.zh: 稜四歲而孤，養母弟以孝友稱。

translations.literal: Leng at four years was orphaned; he raised his mother and younger brother and was praised for filiality and fraternal love.
translations.idiomatic: Han Leng was orphaned at four; he reared his mother and younger brother and was known for filial piety and brotherly devotion.

sentences.zh: 及壯，推先父余財數百萬與從昆弟，鄉里益高之。

translations.literal: When grown, he pushed the several million cash remaining from his late father onto his male cousins of the second degree; the village esteemed him the more.
translations.idiomatic: As an adult he handed several million cash of his father's estate to his cousins, and the locality esteemed him all the more.

sentences.zh: 初為郡功曹，太守葛興中風，病不能聽政，稜陰代興視事，出入二年，令無違者。

translations.literal: At first he was merit assessor of the commandery; grand administrator Ge Xing suffered a stroke and could not hear government; Leng secretly replaced Xing in overseeing affairs; for two years in and out there was none who disobeyed.
translations.idiomatic: He began as county merit assessor; when Governor Ge Xing was paralyzed and could not govern, Han Leng quietly ran the commandery for two years without anyone challenging his authority.

sentences.zh: 興子嘗發教欲署吏，稜拒執不從，因令怨者章之。

translations.literal: Xing's son once issued orders wishing to appoint clerks; Leng resisted and would not comply; therefore he had resentful men memorialize against him.
translations.idiomatic: Ge Xing's son tried to issue appointments; Han Leng blocked him, so enemies filed charges.

sentences.zh: [一]事下案驗，吏以稜掩蔽興病，專典郡職，遂致禁錮。

translations.literal: [1] The matter was sent down for investigation; clerks considered that Leng had concealed Xing's illness and monopolized commandery duties; thereupon he received lifelong disqualification from office.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The case was investigated; the clerks ruled that he had hidden the governor's illness and usurped power, and he was barred from office for life.

sentences.zh: 顯宗知其忠，後詔特原之。

translations.literal: Emperor Xianzong knew his loyalty; later an edict specially pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming knew him loyal and later issued a special pardon.

sentences.zh: 由是征辟，五遷為尚書令，與僕射郅壽﹑尚書陳寵，同時俱以才能稱。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was summoned and appointed; in five promotions he became director of the masters; together with vice director Zhi Shou and master Chen Chong, at the same time all were praised for talent and ability.
translations.idiomatic: He was then recruited and rose in five steps to director of the masters alongside vice director Zhi Shou and Master Chen Chong—all three celebrated for ability.

sentences.zh: 肅宗嘗賜諸尚書□，唯此三人特以寶□，自手署其名曰：「韓稜楚龍淵，[二]郅壽蜀漢文，陳寵濟南椎成。

translations.literal: Suzong once gifted the various masters of writing with swords; only these three he specially gifted precious blades, personally inscribing their names, saying: "Han Leng, Chu Longyuan; [2] Zhi Shou, Shu Hanwen; Chen Chong, Jinan Zhuicheng.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang once gave swords to the masters of writing; to these three alone he gave blades of fine steel and inscribed their names himself: "Han Leng—Dragon Pool of Chu," [2] "Zhi Shou—Han pattern of Shu," "Chen Chong—Hammer Forged of Jinan."

sentences.zh: 」[三]時論者為之說：以稜淵深有謀，故得龍淵；

translations.literal: " [3] Contemporary debaters explained it: because Leng was deep as a pool and had stratagems, therefore he obtained Longyuan;
translations.idiomatic: [3] Critics explained: Han Leng was deep and resourceful as the Dragon Pool spring;

sentences.zh: 壽明達有文章，故得漢文；

translations.literal: Shou was bright and reached and had literary pattern, therefore he obtained Hanwen;
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Shou was lucid and literary, hence the Shu blade;

sentences.zh: 寵敦樸，善不見外，故得椎成。

translations.literal: Chong was honest and plain, good not showing outwardly, therefore he obtained Zhuicheng.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Chong was sturdy and plain, his virtue not flashy, hence the hammer-forged sword.

sentences.zh: 注[一]章謂令上章告言之。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Zhang means to order upward memorial to report.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Here the word means to file a formal memorial of accusation.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《晉大康記》曰：「汝南西平縣有龍泉水，可淬刀□，特堅利。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Jin Taikang Record says: "In Xiping county of Runan is Dragon Spring water, which can temper blades to be especially hard and sharp."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Jin Taikang Record says the Dragon Spring at Xiping in Runan hardened steel for especially keen blades.

sentences.zh: 」汝南即楚分野。

translations.literal: " Runan is Chu's field division.
translations.idiomatic: Runan lay in the old astrological field of Chu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]椎音直追反。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Chui is read with fanqie zhi zhui.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The commentary gives the fanqie reading for the syllable in the sword name.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀「椎成」作「鍛成」。

translations.literal: Han Official Rites writes 椎成 as 鍛成.
translations.idiomatic: The Han Official Rites variant reads "forged complete" instead of "hammer complete."

sentences.zh: 注[一]易下系之辭也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] It is language from the lower wing of the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The allusion comes from the lower part of the Book of Changes.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書音義曰：「城旦，輕刑之名也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Former Documents sound-meanings says: "Chengdan is the name of a light punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The Former Han gloss defines chengdan as a lighter penal labor.

sentences.zh: 晝日司寇虜，夜暮築長城，故曰城旦。」

translations.literal: By day they guard as convict laborers, by night they build the Long Wall; therefore it is called chengdan."
translations.idiomatic: By day they served as corvée guards, by night they built the wall—hence the term."

sentences.zh: 遷南陽太守，特聽稜得過家上頤，鄉里以為榮。

translations.literal: He was transferred grand administrator of Nanyang; he was specially permitted Leng to pass his home to ascend the tomb mound; the village took it as glory.
translations.idiomatic: As grand administrator of Nanyang he was specially allowed to visit his family tomb on the way; his neighbors counted it an honor.

sentences.zh: 稜發擿奸盜，郡中震慄，政號嚴平。

translations.literal: Leng exposed and dragged out traitors and thieves; the commandery trembled; his government was called strict and even.
translations.idiomatic: He rooted out bandits and thieves until the commandery shook; his rule was known as strict but fair.

sentences.zh: 數歲，征入為太僕。

translations.literal: After several years he was summoned in as grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: After a few years he was recalled as grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 九年冬，代張奮為司空。

translations.literal: In the ninth year, winter, he replaced Zhang Fen as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth year, winter, he succeeded Zhang Fen as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 明年薨。

translations.literal: The next year he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died the following year.

sentences.zh: 子輔，安帝時至趙相。

translations.literal: His son Fu, in Emperor An's time reached chancellor of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: His son Han Fu became chancellor to the king of Zhao under Emperor An.
Editorial note [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]趙王良孫商之相也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Chancellor to Liu Shang, king of Zhao, grandson of Prince Liang of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He was chancellor to Liu Shang, king of Zhao, a grandson of Prince Liang of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 稜孫演

translations.literal: Leng's grandson Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan, grandson of Han Leng.

sentences.zh: 稜孫演，順帝時為丹陽太守，政有能名。

translations.literal: Leng's grandson Yan, in Shundi's time was grand administrator of Danyang; his government had a reputation for competence.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan served as grand administrator of Danyang under Emperor Shun and was known for capable rule.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時為司徒。

translations.literal: In Huandi's time he was minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Huan he became minister of education.

sentences.zh: [一]大將軍梁冀被誅，演坐阿黨抵罪，以減死論，遣歸本郡。

translations.literal: [1] When great general Liang Ji was executed, Yan sat for currying faction and answered for crime; by commuted death sentence he was sent back to his native commandery.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When Grand General Liang Ji fell, Han Yan was convicted as his partisan and spared only exile to his home commandery.

sentences.zh: [二]後復征拜司隸校尉。

translations.literal: [2] Later he was again summoned and appointed metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He was later recalled as metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]演字伯南。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yan's courtesy name was Bonan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Han Yan's style was Bonan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰「梁皇后崩，梁貴人大幸，將立，大將軍冀欲分其寵，謀冒姓為貴人父，演陰許諾，及冀誅事發，演坐抵罪」也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Hua Qiao's book says "When Empress Liang died, the Liang imperial lady was greatly favored and was about to be established; great general Ji wished to divide her favor and plotted falsely to claim surname as the lady's father; Yan secretly promised; when Ji was executed the affair came out and Yan sat answering for crime."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Hua Qiao records that after Empress Liang died, another Liang lady was favored for empress; Liang Ji wanted a rival and had Han Yan pose as her father; when Ji fell, Han Yan was implicated."

sentences.zh: 周榮字平孫，廬江舒人也。

translations.literal: Zhou Rong's courtesy name was Pingsun; he was a man of Shu in Lujiang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Rong, style Pingsun, came from Shu in Lujiang.

sentences.zh: 肅宗時，舉明經，辟司徒袁安府。

translations.literal: In Suzong's time he was recommended on the classics and summoned to minister of education Yuan An's office.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhang he passed the classics examination and entered Minister Yuan An's staff.

sentences.zh: 安數與論議，甚器之。

translations.literal: An repeatedly debated with him and greatly valued him.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan An debated policy with him often and thought highly of him.

sentences.zh: 及安舉奏竇景及與竇憲爭立北單于事，皆榮所具草。

translations.literal: When An memorialized against Dou Jing and in disputing with Dou Xian over establishing the northern shanyu, all were drafts supplied by Rong.
translations.idiomatic: The memorials impeaching Dou Jing and opposing Dou Xian's northern shanyu plan were all drafted by Zhou Rong.

sentences.zh: 竇氏客太尉掾徐齮深惡之，脅榮曰：「子為袁公腹心之謀，排奏竇氏，竇氏悍士刺客滿城中，謹備之矣！

translations.literal: Dou clan retainer grand commandant's clerk Xu Qi deeply hated him and threatened Rong, saying: "You are the belly-and-heart plotter for Lord Yuan, drafting memorials to expel the Dou clan; Dou bravos and assassins fill the city—take care!"
translations.idiomatic: The Dou retainer Xu Qi, clerk to the grand commandant, hated him and snarled, "You are Yuan An's secret pen against the Dous; their bravos and killers pack the capital—watch yourself!"

sentences.zh: 」榮曰：「榮江淮孤生，蒙先帝大恩，以歷宰二城。

translations.literal: " Rong said: "Rong is a lone sprout from the Yangzi and Huai; I received the late emperor's great grace and have governed two cities.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Rong replied, "I am a nobody from the south, favored by the late emperor and trusted in two magistracies.

sentences.zh: 今復得備宰士，[一]縱為竇氏所害，誠所甘心。

translations.literal: Now again I obtain to prepare as ministerial clerk; [1] even if harmed by the Dou clan, I would sincerely be willing."
translations.idiomatic: [1] Now I serve on a minister's staff—even if the Dous kill me, I accept it gladly."

sentences.zh: 」故常□妻子，若卒遇飛禍，無得殯斂，[二]冀以區區腐身覺悟朝廷。

translations.literal: " Therefore he often instructed his wife and children that if he suddenly met flying disaster they must not bury him in full rites; [2] he hoped by this petty body's rotting to awaken the court.
translations.idiomatic: " He often warned his family that if assassins struck he was not to be given a full funeral; [2] he was willing to die anonymously to wake the court to the danger."

sentences.zh: 及竇氏敗，榮由此顯名。

translations.literal: When the Dou clan fell, Rong thereby became famous.
translations.idiomatic: When the Dous fell he became famous for his courage.

sentences.zh: 自郾令擢為尚書令。

translations.literal: From magistrate of Yan he was raised to director of the masters.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from magistrate of Yan to director of the masters.

sentences.zh: 出為穎川太守，坐法，當下獄，和帝思榮忠節，左轉共令。

translations.literal: He went out as grand administrator of Yingchuan; sitting for a legal offense he ought to have entered prison; He Emperor thinking of Rong's loyal integrity demoted him to magistrate of Gong.
translations.idiomatic: As grand administrator of Yingchuan he broke the law and should have been jailed; Emperor He remembered his loyalty and only demoted him to magistrate of Gong.

sentences.zh: [三]歲餘，復以為山陽太守。

translations.literal: [3] After more than a year he again was made grand administrator of Shanyang.
translations.idiomatic: [3] A year later he was again named grand administrator of Shanyang.

sentences.zh: 所歷郡縣，皆見稱紀。

translations.literal: In every commandery and county he passed through he was praised in records.
translations.idiomatic: Everywhere he served he left a good name in the registers.

sentences.zh: 以老病乞身，卒於家，詔特賜錢二十萬，除子男興為郎中。

translations.literal: Begging his person on old age and illness, he died at home; an edict specially granted two hundred thousand cash and appointed his son Xing gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He asked to retire for age and illness and died at home; the court gave two hundred thousand cash and appointed his son Zhou Xing a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 注[一]榮辟司徒府，故稱宰士。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Rong was summoned to the minister of education's office; therefore he is called ministerial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: He was on the minister of education's staff, hence "ministerial clerk."

sentences.zh: 注[二]飛禍言倉卒而死也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Flying disaster means dying suddenly.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: "Flying disaster" means sudden death.

sentences.zh: 注[三]共，縣名，屬河內郡，故城在今衛州共城縣東，即古共國也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Gong is a county name, belonging to Henei commandery; the old walled town is today east of Gongcheng county in Weizhou, which was ancient Gong state.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Gong was a county in Henei; its old seat lay east of present Gongcheng in Weizhou, the ancient state of Gong.

sentences.zh: 興少有名譽，永寧中，尚書陳忠上疏薦興曰：「臣伏惟古者帝王有所號令，言必弘雅，辭必溫麗，垂於後世，列於典經。

translations.literal: Xing in youth had a reputation; in Yongning, Master of Writing Chen Zhong submitted memorial recommending Xing, saying: "Your servant humbly thinks that in antiquity when emperors and kings issued commands, their words had to be grand and elegant, their phrasing warm and beautiful, hanging for later ages, arrayed in canonical classics.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xing was known young; in the Yongning era Chen Zhong of the masters office memorialized: "Ancient sovereigns phrased edicts in lofty, polished language meant to endure in the canon.

sentences.zh: 故仲尼嘉唐虞之文章，從周室之鬱鬱。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhongni praised Yao and Shun's pattern, followed the Zhou house's luxuriant richness.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius praised the splendor of Yao and Shun and admired the rich culture of Zhou."

sentences.zh: [一]臣竊見光祿郎周興，[二]孝友之行，著於閨門，清厲之志，聞於州裡。

translations.literal: [1] Your servant has seen Supervisor of the Household Zhou Xing; [2] filial and fraternal conduct is manifest in the inner quarters; pure and stern resolve is heard in the village.
translations.idiomatic: [1] I nominate Zhou Xing of the supervisor of the household office; [2] his filial piety is known at home, his integrity in the district."

sentences.zh: 蘊橋古今，博物多聞，[三]三墳之篇，五典之策，無所不覽。

translations.literal: He stores ancient and modern in the chest; broadly hears and knows much; [3] the chapters of the Three Tombs, the policies of the Five Canons—there is nothing he has not perused.
translations.idiomatic: [3] He has mastered antiquity and the present, read every classic from the Three Tombs to the Five Canons, and commands a scholar's breadth."

sentences.zh: [四]屬文著辭，有可觀采。

translations.literal: [4] When he composes texts and sets down phrases, there is something worth observing and gathering.
translations.idiomatic: [4] His memorials are worth publishing."

sentences.zh: 尚書出納帝命，為王喉舌。

translations.literal: The masters office receives and issues the emperor's commands; it is the king's throat and tongue.
translations.idiomatic: The masters office is the sovereign's voice—it receives and issues the imperial will."

sentences.zh: [五]臣等既愚闇，而諸郎多文俗吏，鮮有雅才，每為詔文，宣示內外，轉相求請，或以不能而專己自由，辭多鄙固。

translations.literal: [5] We your servants are indeed dull, yet many of the gentlemen are vulgar clerks with brush, rarely having elegant talent; whenever they draft edict language to proclaim within and without, they turn and seek one another's help, or because they cannot do it they insist on their own way; the wording is much base and rigid.
translations.idiomatic: [5] We ministers are untalented, and most gentlemen of the office are petty clerks with little literary skill; edicts drafted by them are passed from hand to hand or written stubbornly by the incompetent, and the prose turns crude."

sentences.zh: 興抱奇懷能，隨輩棲□，誠可歎惜。

translations.literal: Xing embraces wonder and cherishes ability, yet with the cohort perches in low rank—truly pitiable.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xing is a man of rare gifts yet stuck among petty colleagues—a waste."

sentences.zh: 」詔乃拜興為尚書郎。

translations.literal: " An edict thereupon appointed Xing gentleman of the masters.
translations.idiomatic: " The emperor appointed Zhou Xing gentleman of the masters.

sentences.zh: 卒。

translations.literal: He died.
translations.idiomatic: He died soon afterward.

sentences.zh: 興子景。

translations.literal: Xing's son Jing.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Zhou Jing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《論語》孔子曰：「大哉堯之為君也，煥乎其有文章。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Analects: Confucius said: "Great indeed was Yao as ruler—how radiant his pattern."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: The Analects quotes Confucius praising Yao's civilizing splendor.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「周監於二代，鬱鬱乎文哉。

translations.literal: " Again he said: "Zhou looked to the two dynasties—how rich in culture.
translations.idiomatic: " He also said, Zhou studied the two earlier dynasties—how rich its culture."

sentences.zh: 吾從周。」

translations.literal: I follow Zhou."
translations.idiomatic: " I follow Zhou."

sentences.zh: 注[二]光祿主郎，故曰光祿郎。

translations.literal: Comment [2] The supervisor of the household presides over gentlemen; therefore he is called supervisor of the household gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: The office oversaw gentlemen of the household, hence the title.

sentences.zh: 注[三]蘊，藏也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] 蘊 means store.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: The first character means to store or accumulate.

sentences.zh: 橋，匱也。

translations.literal: 橋 means chest.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss defines the second character as a chest or storehouse of learning.

sentences.zh: 注[四]伏羲﹑神農﹑黃帝之書曰三墳；

translations.literal: Comment [4] The books of Fuxi, Shennong, and the Yellow Emperor are called the Three Tombs;
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: The Three Tombs are the scriptures attributed to Fuxi, Shennong, and the Yellow Emperor;

sentences.zh: 少昊﹑顓頊﹑高辛﹑唐﹑虞之書曰五典也。

translations.literal: the books of Shaohao, Zhuanxu, Gaoxin, Tang, and Yu are called the Five Canons."
translations.idiomatic: the Five Canons are those of Shaohao, Zhuanxu, Gaoxin, Tang, and Shun."

sentences.zh: 注[五]尚書為王之喉舌官也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] The masters office is the office of the king's throat and tongue.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: The masters office was traditionally called the ruler's "throat and tongue."

sentences.zh: 李固對策曰：「今陛下有尚書，猶天之有北斗也。

translations.literal: Li Gu in answering policy questions said: "Now Your Majesty has the masters office—it is like Heaven's having the Northern Dipper.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu once said in a policy essay, "The masters office is to Your Majesty what the Big Dipper is to Heaven."

sentences.zh: 北斗為天之喉舌，尚書亦為陛下之喉舌也。」

translations.literal: The Dipper is Heaven's throat and tongue; the masters office likewise is Your Majesty's throat and tongue."
translations.idiomatic: The Dipper guides the sky; the masters office guides your government."

sentences.zh: 興子景

translations.literal: Xing's son Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Jing, son of Zhou Xing.

sentences.zh: 景字仲饗。

translations.literal: Jing's courtesy name was Zhongxiang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Jing bore the courtesy name Zhongxiang.

sentences.zh: 辟大將軍梁冀府，稍遷豫州刺史﹑河內太守。

translations.literal: He was summoned to great general Liang Ji's office, gradually rose to provincial governor of Yu and grand administrator of Henei.
translations.idiomatic: He entered Grand General Liang Ji's staff, then rose to governor of Yu province and grand administrator of Henei.

sentences.zh: 好賢愛士，其拔才薦善，常恐不及。

translations.literal: He loved worthies and cherished gentlemen; in raising talent and recommending the good he always feared not to reach enough.
translations.idiomatic: He loved men of talent and strained to recommend the worthy, always fearing he had missed someone.

sentences.zh: 每至歲時，延請舉吏入上後堂，與共宴會，如此數四，乃遣之。

translations.literal: Whenever the year's seasons came, he extended invitation to nominated clerks to enter the rear hall above, and with them shared banquets—thus several times—then sent them off.
translations.idiomatic: Each year he invited nominated clerks to his rear hall, feasted them several times, and only then let them go.

sentences.zh: 贈送什物，無不充備。

translations.literal: Gifts of assorted goods—there was none not fully supplied.
translations.idiomatic: He sent them off with lavish parting gifts.

sentences.zh: 既而選其父兄子弟，事相優異。

translations.literal: Presently he selected their fathers, elder brothers, sons, and younger brothers and treated their affairs with special favor.
translations.idiomatic: He then favored their relatives in office as well.

sentences.zh: 常稱曰：「臣子同貫，若之何不厚！

translations.literal: He often said: "Ministers and sons share one thread—how can we not treat them generously!"
translations.idiomatic: He used to say, "Officials and their kin are one household—why stint them?"

sentences.zh: 」先是司徒韓演在河內，志在無私，舉吏當行，一辭而已，恩亦不及其家。

translations.literal: " Before that, minister of education Han Yan had been in Henei; his intent was selfless; when nominating clerks about to depart he gave one farewell only; favor also did not reach their families.
translations.idiomatic: His predecessor Han Yan as minister of education had governed Henei with cold justice: one brief farewell to outgoing nominees and no favors for their families.

sentences.zh: 曰：「我舉若可矣，豈可令□積一門！

translations.literal: He said: "If I have raised you, that suffices—how can we let □ accumulate in one gate!"
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan had said, "My nomination is enough honor—why heap every favor on one clan?"

sentences.zh: 」故當時論者議此二人。

translations.literal: " Therefore debaters of the time discussed these two men.
translations.idiomatic: " The age compared the two styles of patronage.

sentences.zh: 景後征入為將作大匠。

translations.literal: Jing later was summoned in as court architect.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Jing was later recalled as court architect.

sentences.zh: 及梁冀誅，景以故吏免官禁錮。

translations.literal: When Liang Ji was executed, Jing as former clerk was dismissed from office and disqualified.
translations.idiomatic: When Liang Ji fell he was stripped as a former client and barred from office.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以景素著忠正，頃之，復引拜尚書令。

translations.literal: The court because Jing had always been noted loyal and upright, shortly afterward again summoned and appointed him director of the masters.
translations.idiomatic: The court soon recalled him as director of the masters, knowing his old reputation for integrity.

sentences.zh: [一]遷太僕﹑衛尉。

translations.literal: [1] He was transferred grand coachman and commandant of the guards.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He rose to grand coachman and commandant of the guards.

sentences.zh: 六年，代劉寵為司空。

translations.literal: In the sixth year he replaced Liu Chong as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year he succeeded Liu Chong as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 是時宦官任人及子弟充塞列位。

translations.literal: At that time eunuchs' appointees and their sons and younger brothers filled every rank.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs and their kin had packed the bureaucracy.

sentences.zh: 景初視事，與太尉楊秉舉奏諸奸猾，自將軍牧守以下，免者五十餘人。

translations.literal: When Jing first took up affairs, together with grand commandant Yang Bing he memorialized against the various crafty villains; from generals, shepherds, and keepers downward, those dismissed were more than fifty persons.
translations.idiomatic: On taking office he and Grand Commandant Yang Bing impeached dozens of corrupt officials from generals and governors down.

sentences.zh: 遂連及中常侍防東侯覽﹑東武陽侯具瑗，皆坐黜。

translations.literal: Thereupon it connected to palace attendant marquis of Fangdong Lan and marquis of Dongwuyang Ju Yuan—both sat and were demoted.
